title: Contributing to MockData
text: R (>= 4.2.0) RStudio another R IDE (recommended) Git Package development tools: install.packages(c("devtools", "testthat", "roxygen2"))

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Fork clone repository: Double-click MockData.Rproj : File → Open Project → Select MockData.Rproj Install development dependencies: Load package:
code: git clone https://github.com/your-username/MockData.git cd MockData devtools::install_dev_deps() devtools::load_all()

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Create new branch work: Make changes following coding standards Document changes: Update function documentation (roxygen2 comments) Add examples appropriate Update NEWS.md changes Write tests new functionality: Add tests tests/testthat/test-mockdata.R Ensure tests pass: devtools::test() Run checks: Commit changes following commit message guidelines Push create pull request
code: git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name devtools::check()

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Use 2 spaces indentation (tabs) Line length: aim < 80 characters, max 100 Use <- assignment, = Function names: snake_case Variable names: snake_case Constants: SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE

title: Contributing to MockData
text: exported functions must roxygen2 documentation Include @param, @return, @examples, @export tags Examples runnable (use \dontrun{} sparingly) Use Canadian spelling (behaviour, colour, centre)

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Follow Canadian Government Digital Standards: - Format: type: brief description - Types: - feat: New feature - fix: Bug fix - docs: Documentation changes - test: Adding updating tests - refactor: Code refactoring - style: Code style changes (formatting, etc.) - chore: Maintenance tasks Examples: credit AI tools commit messages (per project guidelines).
code: feat: add support for date variable generation fix: handle missing NA codes in categorical variables docs: update README with CHMS example test: add tests for parse_range_notation edge cases

title: Contributing to MockData
code: # Run all tests devtools::test()  # Run specific test file testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test-mockdata.R")  # Run with coverage covr::package_coverage()

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Place tests tests/testthat/test-mockdata.R Use descriptive test names: test_that("parse_range_notation handles closed intervals", { ... }) Test edge cases error conditions Aim high code coverage

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Use existing metadata inst/extdata/ tests adding new test data, document purpose Keep test data small focused

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Install Quarto: Download https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/ use Homebrew: Ensure pkgdown roxygen2 available: Configure PATH IDE users (Positron/RStudio): encounter “System command ‘quarto’ failed” errors, add Quarto PATH ~/.Renviron: restart R session.
code: brew install quarto # These are in Suggests, install if needed renv::install(c("pkgdown", "roxygen2")) PATH="/usr/local/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin:${PATH}"

title: Contributing to MockData
text: Important: Always run roxygen2::roxygenize() pkgdown::build_site() ’ve modified roxygen documentation R files.
code: # 1. Regenerate .Rd files from roxygen comments (after editing R documentation) roxygen2::roxygenize()  # 2. Build the pkgdown site pkgdown::build_site()  # 3. Preview locally browseURL("docs/index.html")

title: Advanced topics
text: guide covers advanced technical topics MockData power users, including: Derived variables custom functions Unique identifiers (UIDs) traceability Multi-database workflows Integration harmonization pipelines Performance optimization Prerequisites: Read Getting started guide document.

title: Advanced topics
text: Derived variables calculated variables using custom functions. MockData identifies skips derived variables generation, leaving post-generation calculation. Derived variables use special patterns variable_details.csv: recStart contains DerivedVar::[var1, var2, ...] recEnd contains Func::function_name Example: generating mock data, calculate derived variables using custom functions: Separation concerns: Raw data generation vs. business logic Reusability: derivation logic mock real data Testing: Verify derivation functions known inputs Documentation: Metadata explicitly documents dependencies
code: uid,uid_detail,variable,recStart,recEnd,catLabel cchsflow_v0006,cchsflow_d00016,BMI_derived,"DerivedVar::[height, weight]","Func::bmi_fun","BMI calculated from height and weight" # During create_mock_data(), derived variables are excluded enabled_vars <- variables[grepl("enabled", variables$role), ]  # Identify derived variables derived_vars <- identify_derived_vars(enabled_vars, variable_details)  # Exclude from generation enabled_vars <- enabled_vars[!enabled_vars$variable %in% derived_vars, ]  # Result: Only raw variables (height, weight) are generated # BMI_derived must be calculated separately after generation # 1. Generate raw variables mock_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000 ) # Result: Contains height, weight (but not BMI_derived)  # 2. Calculate derived variables mock_data$BMI_derived <- bmi_fun(   height = mock_data$height,   weight = mock_data$weight )  # 3. Verify summary(mock_data$BMI_derived) # Define custom function bmi_fun <- function(height, weight) {   # BMI = weight (kg) / height (m)^2   ifelse(     is.na(height) | is.na(weight) | height <= 0,     NA_real_,     weight / (height^2)   ) }  # Apply to mock data mock_data <- mock_data %>%   mutate(BMI_derived = bmi_fun(height, weight))

title: Advanced topics
text: MockData uses unique identifiers track variables categories throughout metadata lifecycle. UIDs provide traceability version control harmonization workflows. Variable-level UIDs (uid column variables.csv): Detail-level UIDs (uid_detail column variable_details.csv): Variable UIDs: Format: {project}_{version}{number} Examples: cchsflow_v0001, ices_v01, chmsflow_v0042 Detail UIDs: Format: {project}_d{number} Examples: cchsflow_d00001, ices_d003, chmsflow_d00156 Traceability: Track variable definitions across metadata versions Version control: Identify variables changed Cross-referencing: Link variables across databases Debugging: Quickly locate specific category rules Documentation: Permanent identifiers scientific publications
code: uid,variable,variableShortLabel,rType cchsflow_v0001,age,Age at interview,integer cchsflow_v0002,smoking,Smoking status,factor ices_v01,interview_date,Interview date,date uid,uid_detail,variable,recStart,recEnd,catLabel cchsflow_v0001,cchsflow_d00001,age,"[18,100]","copy","Valid age range" cchsflow_v0001,cchsflow_d00002,age,"997","NA::b","Don't know" cchsflow_v0002,cchsflow_d00005,smoking,"1","1","Never smoker" cchsflow_v0002,cchsflow_d00006,smoking,"2","2","Former smoker" # Load current metadata variables_v2 <- read.csv("variables_v2.csv")  # Load previous metadata variables_v1 <- read.csv("variables_v1.csv")  # Find variables that changed between versions changed_vars <- anti_join(variables_v2, variables_v1, by = c("uid", "rType"))  # Result: Variables with same UID but different specifications

title: Advanced topics
text: MockData supports generating data multiple databases/cycles using databaseStart parameter. enables testing harmonization code across survey cycles. databaseStart column variable_details.csv specifies databases category applies : Single database: Multiple databases: Different databases may different category codes variable: MockData automatically filters correct rules based databaseStart. Test harmonization: Verify code works across survey cycles Compare databases: Generate comparable mock datasets Version management: Track database-specific variations Batch generation: Create test data entire project
code: uid,uid_detail,variable,databaseStart,recStart,recEnd,catLabel ices_v01,ices_d001,interview_date,minimal-example,"[2001-01-01,2005-12-31]","copy","Interview date range" cchsflow_v0001,cchsflow_d00001,age,"cchs2001_p,cchs2005_p","[18,100]","copy","Valid age range" # Generate for specific database mock_cchs2001 <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000 ) # Generate for multiple cycles databases <- c("cchs2001_p", "cchs2005_p", "cchs2009_p")  mock_data_list <- lapply(databases, function(db) {   create_mock_data(     databaseStart = db,     variables = variables,     variable_details = variable_details,     n = 1000,     seed = 123  # Same seed for consistency   ) })  names(mock_data_list) <- databases uid,uid_detail,variable,databaseStart,recStart,recEnd,catLabel cchsflow_v0002,cchsflow_d00005,smoking,cchs2001_p,"1","1","Never smoker" cchsflow_v0002,cchsflow_d00006,smoking,cchs2001_p,"2","2","Former smoker" cchsflow_v0002,cchsflow_d00007,smoking,cchs2005_p,"01","1","Never smoker" cchsflow_v0002,cchsflow_d00008,smoking,cchs2005_p,"02","2","Former smoker"

title: Advanced topics
text: generator functions check variable already exists creating : Without duplicate checking: duplicate checking: Current approach (explicit control): Pro: Explicit control data frame construction Pro: NULL return signals “variable exists” (useful debugging) Pro: Compatible standalone batch generation modes Con: Requires (!.null(col)) df <- cbind(df, col) pattern Note: create_mock_data() handles internally, users won’t need worry duplicate checking.
code: # From create_cat_var.R (lines 174-178) if (!is.null(df_mock) && var %in% names(df_mock)) {   return(NULL) } # Dangerous - creates duplicate columns for (i in 1:3) {   df <- cbind(df, create_cat_var("SMK_01", ...)) } # Result: df has SMK_01, SMK_01.1, SMK_01.2 # Safe - only creates variable once for (i in 1:3) {   col <- create_cat_var("SMK_01", ..., df_mock = df)   if (!is.null(col)) df <- cbind(df, col) } # Result: df has SMK_01 (created once, subsequent calls return NULL)

title: Advanced topics
text: MockData designed work CCHS/CHMS harmonization ecosystem (cchsflow, chmsflow). Metadata preparation: Use recodeflow metadata format (variables.csv, variable_details.csv) Mock data generation: Use MockData create test datasets Harmonization development: Test harmonization code mock data Validation: Verify harmonization logic applying real data Production: Apply harmonization real CCHS/CHMS data Faster development: need access restricted data testing Reproducible testing: mock data every time (use seed parameter) Edge case testing: Easy create extreme scenarios (prop_garbage parameter) Documentation: Mock data examples clarify harmonization logic CI/CD integration: Automated testing without data access restrictions
code: # 1. Generate mock raw data mock_raw <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 123 )  # 2. Apply harmonization using rec_with_table() harmonized <- rec_with_table(   data = mock_raw,   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p" )  # 3. Validate harmonization logic library(testthat)  test_that("harmonization handles NA codes correctly", {   # Check that missing codes (996-999) are converted to NA   expect_true(all(harmonized$smoking %in% c(1, 2, 3, NA)))   expect_false(any(harmonized$smoking %in% c(996, 997, 998, 999), na.rm = TRUE)) })  test_that("age range is valid", {   valid_ages <- harmonized$age[!is.na(harmonized$age)]   expect_true(all(valid_ages >= 18 & valid_ages <= 100)) })

title: Advanced topics
text: Faster development: need access restricted data testing Reproducible testing: mock data every time (use seed parameter) Edge case testing: Easy create extreme scenarios (prop_garbage parameter) Documentation: Mock data examples clarify harmonization logic CI/CD integration: Automated testing without data access restrictions

title: Advanced topics
text: large-scale mock data generation: 1. Generate batches: 2. Simplify distributions: 3. Minimize metadata: Large datasets (>1M rows) may slow Complex metadata many variables requires processing Normal distributions slower uniform continuous variables
code: # Instead of one large generation result <- create_con_var(..., n = 1000000)  # Generate in batches batch_size <- 100000 batches <- ceiling(1000000 / batch_size)  result_list <- lapply(1:batches, function(i) {   create_con_var(     ...,     n = batch_size,     df_mock = data.frame(id = ((i-1)*batch_size + 1):(i*batch_size))   ) })  result <- bind_rows(result_list) # Uniform is faster than normal (for continuous variables) distribution = "uniform"  # Faster distribution = "normal"    # Slower (normal distribution centered at range midpoint) # Only include variables you need variable_details_subset <- variable_details %>%   filter(variable %in% needed_vars)

title: Advanced topics
text: Use different seeds different variables ensure independence maintaining reproducibility: use different seeds: Ensures variables statistically independent Prevents unwanted correlations variables Maintains reproducibility (seed = data every time) Recommended seed strategy: Document seeds: Always document seed values used data generation code comments project documentation. ensures others can reproduce exact mock datasets.
code: # Generate multiple date variables with different seeds birth_dates <- create_date_var(   var = "birth_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 100 )  death_dates <- create_date_var(   var = "death_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 101  # Different seed ensures independence )  diagnosis_dates <- create_date_var(   var = "diagnosis_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 102  # Different seed ) # Use sequential seeds starting from a base value base_seed <- 1000  mock_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 5000,   seed = base_seed )  # For individual variable generation: # var1: seed = base_seed + 0 (1000) # var2: seed = base_seed + 1 (1001) # var3: seed = base_seed + 2 (1002)

title: MockData for recodeflow users
text: Use mock data test cchsflow chmsflow harmonisation accessing real data: Write debug analysis scripts data access: Generate safe, non-sensitive data training:
code: library(cchsflow)  # 1. Generate mock raw data mock_raw <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = "variables.csv",   variable_details = "variable_details.csv",   n = 1000 )  # 2. Apply harmonisation mock_harmonised <- rec_with_table(   data = mock_raw,   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p" )  # 3. Test your analysis code mock_harmonised %>%   filter(age_der >= 65) %>%   summarise(prevalence_bmi_obesity = mean(bmi_der_cat == "Obese", na.rm = TRUE)) library(dplyr)  # Generate mock data mock_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = "variables.csv",   variable_details = "variable_details.csv",   n = 5000,   seed = 789 )  # Develop analysis pipeline results <- mock_data %>%   filter(!is.na(age), !is.na(smoking)) %>%   group_by(smoking) %>%   summarise(     n = n(),     mean_age = mean(age),     sd_age = sd(age)   )  # Test visualisations ggplot(mock_data, aes(x = age, fill = smoking)) +   geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +   labs(title = "Age distribution by smoking status (MOCK DATA)") # Generate training dataset training_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = "variables.csv",   variable_details = "variable_details.csv",   n = 500,   seed = 111 )  # Save for training exercises write.csv(training_data, "training_cchs_mock.csv", row.names = FALSE)

title: Getting started with MockData
text: MockData generates fake data variable specifications, published “Table 1 - Description study data”. tutorial shows three approaches, simplest powerful. approaches build recodeflow approach using two “worksheets” describe variables used project: variables.csv (data frame) variable_details.csv: Generate single variable using worksheets manually defining mock data specifications Generate variable using mock data configuration added worksheets Generate complete dataset configuration files

what: Approach 1: Single variable with inline specifications
title: Getting started with MockData
text: Let’s generate smoking status variable three categories: happened: started standard recodeflow worksheets, added proportion Called create_cat_var() generate 100 random smoking values Values distributed according specified proportions (50%, 30%, 20%) Used seed reproducibility Limitations approach: “proportions” hardcoded script Difficult maintain multiple variables Better add mock data configurations variables variable_details anything beyond simple examples
code: # Create variables data frame with variable-level metadata variables <- data.frame(   variable = "smoking",   label = "Smoking status",   variableType = "Categorical",   role = "enabled",  # Required: tells MockData to generate this variable   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Create variable_details data frame with category definitions variable_details <- data.frame(   variable = c("smoking", "smoking", "smoking"),   recStart = c("1", "2", "3"),   catLabel = c("Never", "Former", "Current"),   proportion = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2), ### make mock data with these proportions! ###   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Generate smoking variable smoking_data <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "tutorial",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = data.frame(),   n = 100, # how many rows   seed = 123 # to ensure the mock data is reproducible )  # View results head(smoking_data) smoking 1       1 2       2 3       1 4       3 5       3 6       1 table(smoking_data$smoking) 1  2  3 53 29 18

what: Approach 2: Same variable using metadata files
title: Getting started with MockData
text: Instead hardcoding metadata, can read CSV files. makes easier specify proportions maintain consistency: example data paths examples use system.file() load example metadata included MockData package. projects, ’ll use regular file paths: Notice metadata file includes proportions: Never smoker (code 1): 50% Former smoker (code 2): 30% Current smoker (code 3): 17% Don’t know (code 7): 3% improved: Metadata lives CSV files (easy edit, version control) Proportions specified metadata (50%, 30%, 17%, 3%) function call, reads specifications files Generated distribution matches specified proportions
code: # Package examples use: variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE )  # Your code will use: variables <- read.csv(   "path/to/your/variables.csv",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE ) # Read metadata from files config_path <- system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv", package = "MockData") details_path <- system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv", package = "MockData")  # Load the metadata variables_from_file <- read.csv(config_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE) details_from_file <- read.csv(details_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE)  # View the smoking variable specification smoking_details <- details_from_file[details_from_file$variable == "smoking",                                       c("uid_detail", "variable", "recStart", "catLabel", "proportion")] print(smoking_details, row.names = FALSE) uid_detail variable recStart       catLabel proportion  cchsflow_d00005  smoking        1   Never smoker       0.50  cchsflow_d00006  smoking        2  Former smoker       0.30  cchsflow_d00007  smoking        3 Current smoker       0.17  cchsflow_d00008  smoking        7     Don't know       0.03 # Generate using metadata files (pass full data frames) smoking_data_v2 <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "tutorial",   variables = variables_from_file,   variable_details = details_from_file,   df_mock = data.frame(),   n = 1000,   seed = 456 )  # View distribution table(smoking_data_v2$smoking) 1   2   3   7 466 309 195  30 # View proportions round(prop.table(table(smoking_data_v2$smoking)), 2) 1    2    3    7 0.47 0.31 0.20 0.03

title: Configuration reference

Column counts, CSV examples, dataset summaries calculated dynamically : inst/extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv inst/extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv approach ensures documentation stays synchronized package serves integration testing package builds.

title: Configuration reference
text: MockData uses two-file configuration system define mock datasets: variables.csv - Variable-level metadata generation parameters (24 columns total: 4 core + 20 extensions) variable_details.csv - Detail-level specifications categories ranges (7 columns: 4 core + 3 extensions) reference documents complete v0.2.1 schema, including extension columns, interval notation, validation rules.

title: Configuration reference
text: Purpose: Variable-level metadata generation parameters UID format: Use pattern vNNN zero-padded numbers (e.g., v001, v002, v010) Role values: enabled - Generate variable (required generation) predictor - Use regression models outcome - Outcome variable metadata - Metadata/administrative variable table1 - Include Table 1 summary Seed pattern: Recommended: seed = position × 10 (ensures reproducibility prevents correlation artifacts) Two garbage data modes: Advanced (precise control): Use garbage_low_prop/garbage_low_range /garbage_high_prop/garbage_high_range specify exact ranges Simple (auto-generated): Use prop_garbage automatic invalid value generation Precedence: garbage_low_prop garbage_high_prop specified, take precedence. Otherwise, prop_garbage used. Interpretation variable type: Categorical: prop_garbage generates invalid codes (99, 999, 88, etc. valid categories) Continuous: Advanced uses specified ranges; simple generates --range values Date: Advanced uses specified date ranges; simple generates dates 1-5 years /valid range Examples: Sentinel values: Use NA applicable, "[,]" empty ranges. Distribution types: continuous variables: "normal" - Normal (Gaussian) distribution (requires mean, sd) "uniform" - Uniform distribution valid range date variables: "uniform" - Equal probability dates "gompertz" - Age-related hazard (requires rate, shape, followup_min, followup_max, event_prop) "exponential" - Constant hazard (requires rate, followup_min, followup_max, event_prop) categorical variables: Set NA (categories defined proportions variable_details.csv) Examples: use: Date variables representing events (death_date, disease_diagnosis, etc.) index dates (interview_date) - time origin Example: Use cases: Track changes generation parameters time Document garbage data values changed Maintain audit trail reproducibility
code: # Advanced: Age with precise low garbage data (negative ages) uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v001,age,0.01,"[-5,10]",NA,"[,]",NA  # Advanced: BMI with two-sided garbage data (2% low + 1% high) uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v003,BMI,0.02,"[-10,0]",0.01,"[60,150]",NA  # Simple: Smoking with auto-generated invalid codes uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v002,smoking,NA,NA,NA,NA,0.05  # Simple: Death date with auto-generated out-of-period dates uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v006,death_date,NA,"[,]",NA,"[,]",0.02 # Age: normal distribution uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v001,age,normal,50,15,NA,NA  # BMI: normal distribution uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v003,BMI,normal,27.5,5.2,NA,NA  # Interview date: uniform uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v004,interview_date,uniform,NA,NA,NA,NA  # Primary event: gompertz survival uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v005,primary_event_date,gompertz,NA,NA,0.0001,0.1 # Primary event date: 10% experience dementia diagnosis within 1-15 years uid,variable,distribution,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop v005,primary_event_date,gompertz,365,5475,0.1  # Death date: 20% die within 1-20 years (competing risk) uid,variable,distribution,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop v006,death_date,gompertz,365,7300,0.2  # Loss to follow-up: 10% lost within 1-20 years (censoring) uid,variable,distribution,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop v_007,ltfu_date,uniform,365,7300,0.1 title: Configuration reference
text: UID format: Use pattern vNNN zero-padded numbers (e.g., v001, v002, v010)

title: Configuration reference
text: Role values: enabled - Generate variable (required generation) predictor - Use regression models outcome - Outcome variable metadata - Metadata/administrative variable table1 - Include Table 1 summary Seed pattern: Recommended: seed = position × 10 (ensures reproducibility prevents correlation artifacts) Two garbage data modes: Advanced (precise control): Use garbage_low_prop/garbage_low_range /garbage_high_prop/garbage_high_range specify exact ranges Simple (auto-generated): Use prop_garbage automatic invalid value generation Precedence: garbage_low_prop garbage_high_prop specified, take precedence. Otherwise, prop_garbage used. Interpretation variable type: Categorical: prop_garbage generates invalid codes (99, 999, 88, etc. valid categories) Continuous: Advanced uses specified ranges; simple generates --range values Date: Advanced uses specified date ranges; simple generates dates 1-5 years /valid range Examples: Sentinel values: Use NA applicable, "[,]" empty ranges. Distribution types: continuous variables: "normal" - Normal (Gaussian) distribution (requires mean, sd) "uniform" - Uniform distribution valid range date variables: "uniform" - Equal probability dates "gompertz" - Age-related hazard (requires rate, shape, followup_min, followup_max, event_prop) "exponential" - Constant hazard (requires rate, followup_min, followup_max, event_prop) categorical variables: Set NA (categories defined proportions variable_details.csv) Examples: use: Date variables representing events (death_date, disease_diagnosis, etc.) index dates (interview_date) - time origin Example: Use cases: Track changes generation parameters time Document garbage data values changed Maintain audit trail reproducibility
code: # Advanced: Age with precise low garbage data (negative ages) uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v001,age,0.01,"[-5,10]",NA,"[,]",NA  # Advanced: BMI with two-sided garbage data (2% low + 1% high) uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v003,BMI,0.02,"[-10,0]",0.01,"[60,150]",NA  # Simple: Smoking with auto-generated invalid codes uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v002,smoking,NA,NA,NA,NA,0.05  # Simple: Death date with auto-generated out-of-period dates uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range,prop_garbage v006,death_date,NA,"[,]",NA,"[,]",0.02 # Age: normal distribution uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v001,age,normal,50,15,NA,NA  # BMI: normal distribution uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v003,BMI,normal,27.5,5.2,NA,NA  # Interview date: uniform uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v004,interview_date,uniform,NA,NA,NA,NA  # Primary event: gompertz survival uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,rate,shape v005,primary_event_date,gompertz,NA,NA,0.0001,0.1 # Primary event date: 10% experience dementia diagnosis within 1-15 years uid,variable,distribution,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop v005,primary_event_date,gompertz,365,5475,0.1  # Death date: 20% die within 1-20 years (competing risk) uid,variable,distribution,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop v006,death_date,gompertz,365,7300,0.2  # Loss to follow-up: 10% lost within 1-20 years (censoring) uid,variable,distribution,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop v_007,ltfu_date,uniform,365,7300,0.1

title: Configuration reference
text: recStart column specifies input values ranges using either single values interval notation. categorical variables, specify exact category codes: continuous date variables, use interval notation: [min,max] Format: [min,max] comma delimiter Common bracket types: [,b] - Inclusive ends (common) [,b) - Inclusive start, exclusive end Examples: Important: Always use double quotes around interval notation CSV files ensure comma inside brackets treated column delimiter.
code: uid,recStart,catLabel,proportion v002,1,Never smoker,0.50 v002,2,Former smoker,0.30 v002,3,Current smoker,0.20 # Numeric range: age 18 to 100 (inclusive) uid,recStart v001,"[18,100]"  # Date range: interview dates 2001-2005 uid,recStart v004,"[2001-01-01,2005-12-31]"  # Valid range for distribution truncation uid,recStart v003,"[18,40]"

text: categorical variables, specify exact category codes:

title: Configuration reference
text: continuous date variables, use interval notation: [min,max] Format: [min,max] comma delimiter Common bracket types: [,b] - Inclusive ends (common) [,b) - Inclusive start, exclusive end Examples: Important: Always use double quotes around interval notation CSV files ensure comma inside brackets treated column delimiter.
code: # Numeric range: age 18 to 100 (inclusive) uid,recStart v001,"[18,100]"  # Date range: interview dates 2001-2005 uid,recStart v004,"[2001-01-01,2005-12-31]"  # Valid range for distribution truncation uid,recStart v003,"[18,40]"

title: Configuration reference
text: recEnd column classifies codes missing vs. valid categories, enabling automatic missing data generation. Purpose: Distinguishes : Valid response codes (1, 2, 3) Skip codes (6, 96, 996 - question applicable) Missing codes (7-9, 97-99 - don’t know, refusal, stated) Conditional requirement: Required recStart contains missing data codes (6-9, 96-99) enable proper classification. Map input codes using numeric values: Pattern: recStart="1" → recEnd="1" (code maps ) valid skip/applicable codes (question asked due logic): Statistical treatment: Exclude denominator (respondent eligible question) Common codes: 6, 96, 996 (varies survey) don’t know, refusal, stated (question asked valid response): Statistical treatment: Include denominator calculating response rates, exclude numerator Common codes: 7 (don’t know), 8 (refusal), 9 (stated), 97, 98, 99 Range notation: Can use [7,9] → NA::b map multiple codes Use "copy" valid ranges: Pattern: recEnd=“copy” indicates range used -generation Result: generating data, get_variable_categories(include_na=TRUE) returns code “7”, get_variable_categories(include_na=FALSE) returns codes “1”, “2”, “3”. Without recEnd: distinguish : Code 1 (valid response) Code 7 (missing - don’t know) recEnd: Explicit classification enables: Automatic prop_NA parameter handling Correct missing vs. valid proportions Statistical analysis (response rates, prevalence) Validation: recStart contains codes 6-9 96-99 recEnd column missing, validation error instructions.

title: Configuration reference
text: MockData validates configuration files load: Pattern: uid follow format vNNN (e.g., v001, v042), uid_detail follow dNNN (e.g., d001, d156) Uniqueness: uid values unique variables.csv Uniqueness: uid_detail values unique variable_details.csv Foreign keys: uid variable_details exist variables.csv variables.csv: uid, variable, variableType variable_details.csv: uid, uid_detail, variable, recStart Variable name match: variable_details.variable matches variables.variable Column placement: Extension columns correct files (e.g., distribution params variables.csv) Proportions: Sum 1.0 per variable (±0.01 tolerance) Garbage data proportions: 0 1 Garbage data ranges: Use interval notation [min,max] sentinel "[,]" rType values: One : integer, double, factor, date, character, logical distribution values: One : normal, uniform, gompertz, exponential, NA Versioning: mockDataVersion follows semantic versioning (present) Complete grid: cells explicit values (empty strings optional columns - use NA sentinel values)

title: Configuration reference
text: Purpose: Permanent identifiers traceability across metadata versions Format requirements: uid: Pattern vNNN zero-padding (e.g., v001, v042) uid_detail: Pattern dNNN zero-padding (e.g., d001, d156) Zero-padding recommended sorting: v001 v1 Common errors: Edge cases: UIDs must globally unique across databases Reusing UIDs across databases “variable” acceptable requires consistent definitions Changing UID means “new variable” even variable name unchanged Best practices: Start numbering v_001 (v_000) Use sequential numbers easy tracking Document UID assignment logic project README Use project prefixes multi-project repos: cchs_v001, chms_v001 Purpose: Tag variables different purposes analysis Valid values (comma-separated): Examples: Important: Variable generated unless role contains "enabled". Common errors: Multi-role filtering example: Purpose: Control variable generation order ensure reproducible independence position: Generation order (ascending) Use increments 10 (10, 20, 30…) easy insertion Lower numbers generated first seed: Random seed specific variable Recommended: seed = position × 10 (e.g., position 20 → seed 200) Prevents correlation artifacts variables position matters: variables may depend others generated first (though MockData generally handles automatically). seed matters: Using seed variables can create artificial correlations. Different seeds ensure statistical independence. Examples: Common errors: Inserting variables: Purpose: Simulate raw data formats harmonization pipeline testing Column name: Currently sourceFormat minimal-example (documentation shows sourceData) Valid values: Use case: Test date parsing/harmonization logic Examples: Conversion examples: See Working date variables detailed examples. Purpose: Specify values distributed across valid ranges categorical variables: Use NA (categories defined proportions variable_details.csv) continuous variables: date variables: Complete examples: Common errors: Parameter interpretation: Normal: Values drawn N(mean, sd²), truncated recStart range Gompertz: Hazard increases exponentially age (realistic mortality) Exponential: Constant hazard time (random censoring) Visual interpretation (2001-2020 date range): uniform: Median ≈ 2010 (middle) gompertz: Median ≈ 2015-2018 (later years, increasing hazard) exponential: Median ≈ 2003-2007 (earlier years, constant hazard)
code: # ❌ WRONG: Inconsistent padding uid,variable v_1,age v_10,smoking v_2,BMI # Result: Sorts incorrectly (v_1, v_10, v_2)  # ✅ CORRECT: Zero-padded uid,variable v001,age v002,smoking v010,BMI # Result: Sorts correctly (v001, v002, v_010) # Age: predictor for models, show in Table 1 uid,variable,role v001,age,"enabled,predictor,table1"  # Primary outcome: show in Table 1 uid,variable,role v005,dementia_diagnosis,"enabled,outcome,table1"  # Study ID: just metadata uid,variable,role v020,study_id,"enabled,metadata"  # Derived variable: not enabled (calculated post-generation) uid,variable,role v006,BMI_derived,outcome # ❌ WRONG: Forgot "enabled" uid,variable,role v001,age,"predictor,table1" # Result: age will NOT be generated  # ✅ CORRECT uid,variable,role v001,age,"enabled,predictor,table1" # Get only variables for Table 1 table1_vars <- variables[grepl("table1", variables$role), ]  # Get predictors predictors <- variables[grepl("predictor", variables$role), ]  # Get enabled variables for generation enabled_vars <- variables[grepl("enabled", variables$role), ] # Good: Increments of 10, seed = position × 10 uid,variable,position,seed v001,age,10,100 v002,smoking,20,200 v003,BMI,30,300 # ❌ WRONG: Same seed for all variables uid,variable,position,seed v001,age,10,123 v002,smoking,20,123 v003,BMI,30,123 # Result: Variables may be artificially correlated  # ❌ WRONG: Sequential positions (hard to insert) uid,variable,position,seed v001,age,1,10 v002,smoking,2,20 v003,BMI,3,30 # Result: Hard to insert new variable between age and smoking # Original uid,variable,position v001,age,10 v003,BMI,30  # Insert smoking between age and BMI uid,variable,position v001,age,10 v002,smoking,20  # Fits perfectly v003,BMI,30 # Analysis-ready (default) uid,variable,sourceFormat v004,interview_date,analysis  # CSV import simulation uid,variable,sourceFormat v004,interview_date,csv  # SAS import simulation uid,variable,sourceFormat v004,interview_date,sas # CSV to Date dates_csv <- "2001-01-01" as.Date(dates_csv)  # SAS to Date dates_sas <- 14975 as.Date(dates_sas, origin = "1960-01-01") # Continuous: normal distribution uid,variable,variableType,distribution,mean,sd v001,age,Continuous,normal,50,15  # Continuous: uniform distribution uid,variable,variableType,distribution v010,income_bracket,Continuous,uniform  # Categorical: use NA (proportions in variable_details.csv) uid,variable,variableType,distribution v002,smoking,Categorical,  # Date: uniform (index date) uid,variable,variableType,distribution v004,interview_date,Date,uniform  # Date: gompertz survival (event date) uid,variable,variableType,distribution,rate,shape,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop v005,death_date,Date,gompertz,0.0001,0.1,365,7300,0.2 # ❌ WRONG: Normal distribution without mean/sd uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd v001,age,normal,,  # ❌ WRONG: Distribution for categorical variable uid,variable,variableType,distribution v002,smoking,Categorical,normal  # ✅ CORRECT uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd v001,age,normal,50,15 v002,smoking,,

title: Configuration reference
text: Purpose: Permanent identifiers traceability across metadata versions Format requirements: uid: Pattern vNNN zero-padding (e.g., v001, v042) uid_detail: Pattern dNNN zero-padding (e.g., d001, d156) Zero-padding recommended sorting: v001 v1 Common errors: Edge cases: UIDs must globally unique across databases Reusing UIDs across databases “variable” acceptable requires consistent definitions Changing UID means “new variable” even variable name unchanged Best practices: Start numbering v_001 (v_000) Use sequential numbers easy tracking Document UID assignment logic project README Use project prefixes multi-project repos: cchs_v001, chms_v001

title: Configuration reference
text: Purpose: Tag variables different purposes analysis Valid values (comma-separated): Examples: Important: Variable generated unless role contains "enabled". Common errors: Multi-role filtering example:
code: # Age: predictor for models, show in Table 1 uid,variable,role v001,age,"enabled,predictor,table1"  # Primary outcome: show in Table 1 uid,variable,role v005,dementia_diagnosis,"enabled,outcome,table1"  # Study ID: just metadata uid,variable,role v020,study_id,"enabled,metadata"  # Derived variable: not enabled (calculated post-generation) uid,variable,role v006,BMI_derived,outcome # ❌ WRONG: Forgot "enabled" uid,variable,role v001,age,"predictor,table1" # Result: age will NOT be generated  # ✅ CORRECT uid,variable,role v001,age,"enabled,predictor,table1" # Get only variables for Table 1 table1_vars <- variables[grepl("table1", variables$role), ]  # Get predictors predictors <- variables[grepl("predictor", variables$role), ]  # Get enabled variables for generation enabled_vars <- variables[grepl("enabled", variables$role), ]

what: position and seed: Generation order and reproducibility
title: Configuration reference
text: Purpose: Control variable generation order ensure reproducible independence position: Generation order (ascending) Use increments 10 (10, 20, 30…) easy insertion Lower numbers generated first seed: Random seed specific variable Recommended: seed = position × 10 (e.g., position 20 → seed 200) Prevents correlation artifacts variables position matters: variables may depend others generated first (though MockData generally handles automatically). seed matters: Using seed variables can create artificial correlations. Different seeds ensure statistical independence. Examples: Common errors: Inserting variables:
code: # Good: Increments of 10, seed = position × 10 uid,variable,position,seed v001,age,10,100 v002,smoking,20,200 v003,BMI,30,300 # ❌ WRONG: Same seed for all variables uid,variable,position,seed v001,age,10,123 v002,smoking,20,123 v003,BMI,30,123 # Result: Variables may be artificially correlated  # ❌ WRONG: Sequential positions (hard to insert) uid,variable,position,seed v001,age,1,10 v002,smoking,2,20 v003,BMI,3,30 # Result: Hard to insert new variable between age and smoking # Original uid,variable,position v001,age,10 v003,BMI,30  # Insert smoking between age and BMI uid,variable,position v001,age,10 v002,smoking,20  # Fits perfectly v003,BMI,30

title: Configuration reference
text: Visual interpretation (2001-2020 date range): uniform: Median ≈ 2010 (middle) gompertz: Median ≈ 2015-2018 (later years, increasing hazard) exponential: Median ≈ 2003-2007 (earlier years, constant hazard)

title: Configuration reference
text: Q: ’s difference variables.csv variable_details.csv? : variables.csv defines variable-level metadata (one row per variable). variable_details.csv defines detail-level specifications (multiple rows per variable categories/ranges). Think one--many relationship. Q: Can use single CSV file instead two? : . two-file structure comes recodeflow required. enables: Multiple category definitions per variable Clean separation variable-level vs category-level parameters Standardized harmonization workflow integration Q: need fill columns? : . required columns need values. Optional columns use: NA applicable Empty string unknown (though NA preferred) Sentinel values like [,] empty ranges Q: know columns required? : See Quick reference table. Core required columns: variables.csv: uid, variable, variableType variable_details.csv: uid, uid_detail, variable, recStart Q: use Categorical vs Continuous numeric codes? : Categorical: Discrete codes specific meanings (smoking status: 1=never, 2=former, 3=current) Continuous: Numeric measurements scale (age, BMI, income) Rule thumb: calculate mean, use Continuous. calculate proportions, use Categorical. Q: Can Continuous variable integer values? : Yes. Set variableType = "Continuous" rType = "integer". Example: Age years. Q: ’s difference variableType rType? : variableType: Conceptual type recodeflow (Categorical Continuous) - determines generation logic rType: R output data type specified MockData (integer, double, character, date, factor) - determines output format Note: Date variables use variableType = "Continuous" rType = "date". variableType field comes recodeflow uses Categorical/Continuous values. Q: smoking status factor character? : Use rType = "factor" categorical variables. Factors preserve category levels enable proper statistical analysis. Q: ’s difference garbage_low/garbage_high prop_garbage? : Two modes: Example: Age garbage_low_range = "[-5,10]" generates negative ages Example: Categorical prop_garbage = 0.05 gets invalid codes like 99, 999 Precedence: specify garbage_low_prop garbage_high_prop, take precedence. Otherwise prop_garbage used. Q: want garbage data? : test data quality pipelines: Validate data cleaning code catches impossible values Train analysts real-world data quality issues Test edge cases harmonization logic Q: Can low high garbage data? : Yes. Specify garbage_low_prop/garbage_low_range garbage_high_prop/garbage_high_range: Result: 2% BMI < 0, 1% BMI 60-150, rest valid values. Q: ’s difference NA::NA::b? : Survey missing data classification: Example: Pregnancy questions skipped males Statistical treatment: Exclude denominator Example: Respondent refused answer income question Statistical treatment: Include denominator, exclude numerator Q: missing codes count toward proportion sum? : Yes. proportions must sum 1.0, including missing codes: Q: generate missing data continuous/date variables? : Use else recStart NA classification: Result: 5% age values NA (recoded “else” = everything range). Q: Can use ranges missing codes? : Yes: Q: happens normal distribution generates values outside recStart range? : Values truncated recStart range. Example: Result: Normal distribution N(50, 15²) truncated [18, 100]. values < 18 > 100. Q: use gompertz vs exponential survival data? : Gompertz: Age-related events hazard increases time (mortality, dementia, chronic disease) Exponential: Constant hazard events risk doesn’t change time (loss follow-, random censoring) Q: typical gompertz parameters? : mortality elderly cohorts: rate = 0.0001: Baseline hazard shape = 0.1: Hazard acceleration (positive = increasing hazard age) followup_max = 7300: 20 years event_prop = 0.2: 20% experience event Q: date variables value? : Check: using distribution = "uniform"? (Required variation) recStart interval [start,end] single date? set different seeds variable? Q: Must uid variable_details.csv match uid variables.csv exactly? : Yes. foreign key relationship. Every uid variable_details.csv must exist variables.csv. Q: Can multiple variables uid? : . UIDs must unique within variables.csv. Use different UIDs different variables. Q: Can variable_details.csv gaps uid_detail numbering? : Yes. uid_detail values don’t need sequential, just unique: Q: happens reference uid doesn’t exist variables.csv? : Validation error: Q: strict “proportions must sum 1.0” rule? : Tolerance ±0.01. valid: 1.00 ✓ 0.99 ✓ 1.01 ✓ 0.98 ❌ (far 1.0) Q: garbage data proportions count sum? : . Garbage data (garbage_low_prop, garbage_high_prop, prop_garbage) separate population proportions. Example: Q: want unequal category probabilities? : Specify exact proportions variable_details.csv: Q: databaseStart need ? : Database/cycle identifier filtering. Needed : Generating data multiple survey cycles (CCHS 2001, 2005, 2009) Variables database-specific category codes Testing multi-cycle harmonization Q: specify databases row applies ? : Use comma-separated list databaseStart column (variable_details.csv): Row 1 applies databases. Rows 2-3 database-specific (different codes smoking). Q: ’m generating data one database? : Use single database name consistently: generate :
code: uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range v003,BMI,0.02,"[-10,0]",0.01,"[60,150]" uid,recStart,recEnd,proportion v002,1,1,0.50 v002,2,2,0.30 v002,3,3,0.17 v002,7,NA::b,0.03 # Sum = 1.00 ✓ uid,variable,recStart,recEnd,proportion v001,age,"[18,100]","copy", v001,age,"else","NA::b",0.05 # Map codes 997-999 to NA::b uid,recStart,recEnd v001,"[997,999]",NA::b uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd,recStart v001,age,normal,50,15,"[18,100]" distribution,rate,shape,followup_min,followup_max,event_prop gompertz,0.0001,0.1,365,7300,0.2 uid_detail,variable d001,age d_003,age d_042,age # Valid - gaps are OK Error: uid 'v_999' in variable_details.csv not found in variables.csv # Proportions in variable_details.csv must sum to 1.0 uid,recStart,proportion v002,1,0.50 v002,2,0.30 v002,3,0.20 # Sum = 1.00 ✓  # Garbage data in variables.csv is additional uid,prop_garbage v002,0.05 # Result: 95% valid (distributed 50/30/20), 5% invalid codes uid,recStart,catLabel,proportion v002,1,Never smoker,0.60 v002,2,Former smoker,0.25 v002,3,Current smoker,0.15 # Reflects real population distribution uid,variable,databaseStart,recStart v001,age,"cchs2001_p,cchs2005_p","[18,100]" v002,smoking,cchs2001_p,1 v002,smoking,cchs2005_p,01 uid,variable,databaseStart,recStart v001,age,my_study,"[18,100]" v002,smoking,my_study,1 create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "my_study",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details )

title: Configuration reference
text: Error: “Proportions variable ‘smoking’ sum 0.97, expected 1.0” Cause: Category proportions don’t sum 1.0 (±0.01 tolerance) Fix: Check proportions variable_details.csv variable: Error: “uid ‘v_042’ variable_details.csv found variables.csv” Cause: Foreign key violation - referenced uid doesn’t exist Fix: Check typos missing rows: Error: “Required column ‘recStart’ found variable_details.csv” Cause: Missing required column Fix: Add missing column: Error: “Invalid rType value ‘float’ variable ‘BMI’” Cause: rType must one : integer, double, factor, date, character, logical Fix: Use "double" "float": Error: “distribution ‘normal’ requires ‘mean’ ‘sd’ parameters” Cause: Normal distribution missing required parameters Fix: Specify mean sd: Problem: date variables value Possible causes: Used single date instead interval recStart Forgot specify distribution seed variables Fix: Problem: variables generated (empty data frame) Possible causes: variables role = "enabled" databaseStart filter excludes rows variables derived variables Fix: Problem: Proportions don’t match expected distribution Cause: Forgot account missing data proportion Fix: Missing data proportion reduces valid category proportions: Problem: Garbage data appearing Possible causes: Proportion small see sample Wrong column name (garbage_low vs corrupt_low) Precedence issue (prop_garbage ignored garbage_low_prop specified) Fix: Problem: Generation slow large n Cause: Complex distributions (especially normal) slower uniform Solutions: Use uniform distribution appropriate Generate batches Simplify metadata (fewer variables, fewer categories)
code: # Identify the problem var_details %>%   filter(variable == "smoking") %>%   summarize(total = sum(proportion, na.rm = TRUE))  # Fix: Adjust proportions to sum to 1.0 # Find orphaned uids details_uids <- unique(variable_details$uid) vars_uids <- unique(variables$uid) orphans <- setdiff(details_uids, vars_uids) print(orphans)  # Fix: Either add missing uid to variables.csv or fix typo in variable_details.csv # Check what columns exist names(variable_details)  # Add missing column (with default values if needed) variable_details$recStart <- NA # ❌ WRONG uid,variable,rType v003,BMI,float  # ✅ CORRECT uid,variable,rType v003,BMI,double # ❌ WRONG uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd v001,age,normal,,  # ✅ CORRECT uid,variable,distribution,mean,sd v001,age,normal,50,15 # Check recStart uses interval notation uid,variable,recStart v004,interview_date,"[2001-01-01,2005-12-31]"  # Check distribution is specified uid,variable,distribution v004,interview_date,uniform  # Check different seeds uid,variable,seed v004,interview_date,400 v005,event_date,500 # Check which variables are enabled enabled <- variables[grepl("enabled", variables$role), ] nrow(enabled)  # Should be > 0  # Check databaseStart filtering filtered <- variable_details[   grepl(databaseStart, variable_details$databaseStart), ] nrow(filtered)  # Should be > 0 # If you want 50% never smokers in VALID responses: uid,recStart,recEnd,proportion v002,1,1,0.475     # 50% of 95% = 47.5% v002,2,2,0.285     # 30% of 95% = 28.5% v002,3,3,0.19      # 20% of 95% = 19% v002,7,NA::b,0.05  # 5% missing # Sum = 1.00 # Result: Among VALID responses, 50/30/20 split # Increase proportion for testing uid,variable,garbage_high_prop v003,BMI,0.20  # 20% easier to verify than 1%  # Check correct column names (garbage_ not corrupt_) uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range v001,age,0.05,"[-5,10]"  # If using advanced mode, don't specify prop_garbage uid,variable,garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,prop_garbage v001,age,0.05,"[-5,10]",  # Leave prop_garbage empty # Batch generation example batch_size <- 100000 n_batches <- 10  results <- lapply(1:n_batches, function(i) {   create_mock_data(     databaseStart = "my_study",     variables = variables,     variable_details = variable_details,     n = batch_size,     seed = 1000 + i  # Different seed per batch   ) })  final_data <- bind_rows(results)

title: Configuration reference
text: Use case: Smoking status standard survey missing codes Result: 50% never/30% former/17% current smokers, 3% don’t know (NA), plus 5% invalid codes (99, 999) prop_garbage. Use case: Body mass index biologically impossible values QA testing Result: Normal distribution N(27.5, 5.2²) truncated [15, 50], 2% negative BMI values 1% extremely high (60-150) values. Use case: Age survey missing codes uniform distribution Result: 90% valid ages uniformly distributed 18-100, 10% missing codes 997/998/999 (mapped NA). Use case: Interview date time origin survival analysis Result: Uniform distribution dates 2001-2005 (respondents interviewed), output R Date objects. Use case: Primary event (dementia diagnosis) age-related hazard temporal garbage data Interpretation: 10% experience dementia diagnosis within 0-15 years interview Event times follow gompertz distribution (increasing hazard age) 3% impossible future dates (2021-2099) QA testing 90% censored (event) Use case: Smoking status different category codes across survey cycles Result: CCHS 2001: Codes 1/2/3 (numeric) CCHS 2005: Codes 01/02/03 (zero-padded strings) proportions, different source codes map harmonized values 1/2/3 (recEnd column) Use case: BMI calculated post-generation height weight Generation workflow: Note: Derived variables generated create_mock_data(). See Advanced topics: Derived variables details. Use case: Cohort study primary event, death, censoring Interpretation: Index date: Interview 2001-2005 (100% date) Primary event: 10% develop dementia within 0-15 years (gompertz hazard) Competing risk: 20% die within 1-20 years (gompertz hazard) Censoring: 10% lost follow-within 1-20 years (uniform) Administrative: censored 2024-12-31 Result: Realistic competing risks dataset : experience primary event death/censoring die primary event censored (lost follow-administrative) individual can one terminal event See Tutorial: Generating survival data competing risks complete workflow. Use case: Pregnancy question valid skip males/postmenopausal Result: 5% currently pregnant 30% pregnant 60% valid skip (males, postmenopausal females - eligible question) 5% eligible didn’t answer Statistical interpretation: Denominator prevalence: 0.05 + 0.30 + 0.05 = 0.40 (eligible respondents) Prevalence among eligible: 0.05 / 0.40 = 12.5% Response rate: (0.05 + 0.30) / 0.40 = 87.5% Use case: Tracking changes QA testing parameters time Use case: Track metadata evolution Document garbage data proportions changed Reproduce exact mock datasets specific versions Audit trail scientific publications

Generate height and weight mock_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "my_study",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000 ) # Result: Contains height, weight (but NOT BMI_derived)  # 2. Calculate BMI_derived post-generation bmi_fun <- function(height, weight) {   ifelse(     is.na(height) | is.na(weight) | height <= 0,     NA_real_,     weight / (height^2)   ) }  mock_data$BMI_derived <- bmi_fun(mock_data$height, mock_data$weight)

title: Configuration reference
text: 1. Complete grid: Every cell explicit value Use NA applicable Use "" (empty string) value truly unknown Use sentinel values [,] empty ranges implicit defaults - explicit 2. Separation concerns: variables.csv: Variable-level metadata (one row per variable) variable_details.csv: Category/range specifications (multiple rows per variable) Generation logic: R package functions, metadata 3. Traceability: UIDs provide permanent identifiers Versioning columns track metadata evolution Foreign keys link files (uid column) 4. Composability: Variables can generated independently Metadata can shared across databases (databaseStart filtering) Derived variables calculated post-generation 5. Validation-first: Schema validates generation Fail fast clear error messages Sum--one enforcement prevents silent errors creating metadata: Choose naming convention UIDs (project prefix + zero-padded numbers) Decide databaseStart naming (single study vs multi-cycle) Plan versioning strategy (increment mockDataVersion) Document seed strategy (position × 10 recommended) metadata creation: Use zero-padded UIDs (v001 v1) Use increments 10 position (10, 20, 30) Ensure proportions sum 1.0 variable Include “enabled” role variables generate Use interval notation continuous/date ranges Specify distribution parameters needed Add catLabel documentation metadata creation: Validate files load without errors Check foreign keys (variable_details.uid exist variables.uid) Verify proportions sum correctly Test generation small n (100) first Check output matches expected distributions Verify garbage data appears expected Document non-obvious design decisions production use: Freeze metadata version (commit git) Document mockDataVersion README Include seed generation scripts Save generated data metadata version tag Keep audit trail metadata changes

title: Configuration reference
text: existing recodeflow metadata without MockData extension columns: 1. Add required extension columns variables.csv: 2. Add distribution columns (needed): 3. Add recEnd variable_details.csv: 4. Validate test: Key changes v0.2.0: 1. Garbage data column names changed: Migration: 2. Distribution parameters moved variables.csv: v0.1.x, parameters variable_details.csv. v0.2.x, distribution parameters variables.csv. 3. New versioning columns available: Add track metadata changes time.
code: # Minimal additions variables$rType <- "double"  # Or appropriate type variables$role <- "enabled" variables$position <- seq(10, by = 10, length.out = nrow(variables)) variables$seed <- variables$position * 10 # For continuous variables continuous_vars <- variables$variableType == "Continuous" variables$distribution[continuous_vars] <- "uniform"  # For date variables date_vars <- variables$variableType == "Date" variables$distribution[date_vars] <- "uniform" # For continuous/date variables with ranges variable_details$recEnd[is.na(variable_details$recEnd)] <- "copy"  # For categorical variables (map codes to themselves) # Requires manual review to identify missing codes # Load and validate mock_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "your_database",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 100 )  # Verify structure str(mock_data) summary(mock_data) # OLD (v0.1.x) corrupt_low_prop,corrupt_low_range,corrupt_high_prop,corrupt_high_range  # NEW (v0.2.x) garbage_low_prop,garbage_low_range,garbage_high_prop,garbage_high_range # Rename columns names(variables)[names(variables) == "corrupt_low_prop"] <- "garbage_low_prop" names(variables)[names(variables) == "corrupt_low_range"] <- "garbage_low_range" names(variables)[names(variables) == "corrupt_high_prop"] <- "garbage_high_prop" names(variables)[names(variables) == "corrupt_high_range"] <- "garbage_high_range" mockDataVersion,mockDataLastUpdated,mockDataVersionNotes

title: Configuration reference
text: 1. Use simpler distributions: 2. Minimize variables: include variables actually need. variable adds generation time. 3. Batch generation: 4. Simplify metadata: Reduce number categories per variable Use uniform instead normal distributions Minimize garbage data (QA testing, production) n > 10M rows:
code: # Slower distribution,mean,sd normal,50,15  # Faster distribution uniform # Generate in chunks batch_size <- 100000 n_batches <- ceiling(total_n / batch_size)  results <- lapply(1:n_batches, function(i) {   create_mock_data(     databaseStart = "my_study",     variables = variables,     variable_details = variable_details,     n = min(batch_size, total_n - (i-1)*batch_size),     seed = base_seed + i   ) })  final_data <- bind_rows(results) # Generate in batches and write to disk for (i in 1:n_batches) {   batch <- create_mock_data(     databaseStart = "my_study",     variables = variables,     variable_details = variable_details,     n = batch_size,     seed = base_seed + i   )    # Write to disk immediately   write.csv(batch, paste0("mock_data_batch_", i, ".csv"), row.names = FALSE)    # Free memory   rm(batch)   gc() }  # Combine later if needed all_files <- list.files(pattern = "mock_data_batch_.*\\.csv") combined <- do.call(rbind, lapply(all_files, read.csv))

title: Configuration reference
text: See inst/extdata/minimal-example/ complete working example 11 variables (Categorical : 1, Continuous : 10) 26 detail rows demonstrating: 20 variable-level extension columns (plus 4 core recodeflow columns) 3 detail-level extension columns (plus 4 core recodeflow columns) variable types (integer, factor, double, date) garbage data patterns (advanced low/high, simple prop_garbage) distribution types (normal, uniform, gompertz) Survival analysis competing risks UID-based foreign keys Interval notation throughout Complete grid principle (empty strings, explicit NA/sentinels) example validates successfully can used template new projects.

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: MockData provides two core functions generating common variable types: create_cat_var() - Categorical variables (smoking status, education level, disease diagnosis) create_con_var() - Continuous variables (age, weight, blood pressure, income) tutorial focuses basics two variable types. specialized topics, see: Missing data - Survey missing codes NA patterns Garbage data - Data quality testing intentional errors Date variables - Temporal data survival times

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: Categorical variables represent discrete categories specific meanings. Examples: smoking status, education level, disease diagnosis. Let’s generate smoking status minimal-example metadata: example data paths examples use system.file() load example metadata included MockData package. projects, ’ll use regular file paths: proportion column variable_details.csv controls distribution categories: Smoking proportions metadata: Observed distribution: Key insight: observed proportions closely match metadata specifications. MockData samples categories according specified proportions. smoking (cchsflow_v0002), metadata specifies: variables.csv: variableType = "Categorical" rType = "factor" - Output R factor levels variable_details.csv: category row recStart contains category code (“1”, “2”, “3”) proportion must sum 1.0 across categories Proportion validation: Sum = 1 (must 1.0 ± 0.01)
code: # Package examples use: variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE )  # Your code will use: variables <- read.csv(   "path/to/your/variables.csv",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE ) # Load minimal-example metadata variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   check.names = FALSE )  variable_details <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   check.names = FALSE )  # Generate smoking status (categorical variable) smoking <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 123 )  # View structure head(smoking) smoking 1       1 2       2 3       1 4       3 5       3 6       1 str(smoking) 'data.frame':   1000 obs. of  1 variable:  $ smoking: Factor w/ 4 levels "1","2","3","7": 1 2 1 3 3 1 2 3 2 1 ... # Check the distribution table(smoking$smoking) 1   2   3   7 505 305 160  30 prop.table(table(smoking$smoking)) 1     2     3     7 0.505 0.305 0.160 0.030

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: smoking (cchsflow_v0002), metadata specifies: variables.csv: variableType = "Categorical" rType = "factor" - Output R factor levels variable_details.csv: category row recStart contains category code (“1”, “2”, “3”) proportion must sum 1.0 across categories Proportion validation: Sum = 1 (must 1.0 ± 0.01)

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: Continuous variables represent numeric measurements scale. Examples: age, BMI, blood pressure, income. Let’s generate age minimal-example metadata: Continuous variables use statistical distributions generate realistic values:  Distribution parameters metadata: Distribution: normal Mean: 50 Standard deviation: 15 Interpretation: Age drawn normal distribution mean = 50 SD = 15, truncated valid range specified variable_details.csv. Notice peak 999—raw data complex sources like surveys, missing codes often unrealistically high low values. design, can generate unusual patterns data distribution. See Missing data tutorial information. age (cchsflow_v0001), metadata specifies: variables.csv: variableType = "Continuous" rType = "integer" - Output whole numbers distribution = "normal" - Use normal distribution mean = 50, sd = 15 - Distribution parameters variable_details.csv: recStart = "[18,100]" - Valid range (interval notation) proportion = NA - applicable continuous variables normal distribution truncated [18, 100], ensuring invalid ages generated. Let’s generate weight, requires decimal precision:  Weight parameters: Distribution: normal Mean: 75 kg Standard deviation: 15 kg Output type: double (decimal values) Difference age: Weight uses rType = "double" preserve decimal precision (e.g., 72.3, 85.7), age uses rType = "integer" whole numbers (e.g., 45, 67).
code: # Generate age (continuous variable with integer output) age <- create_con_var(   var = "age",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 456 )  # View structure head(age) age 1  30 2  59 3  62 4  29 5  39 6  45 str(age) 'data.frame':   1000 obs. of  1 variable:  $ age: int  30 59 62 29 39 45 60 54 65 59 ... # Check the distribution summary(age$age) Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.   18.00   42.00   52.00  145.52   65.25  999.00 hist(age$age, breaks = 30, main = "Age Distribution", xlab = "Age (years)", col = "lightblue") # Generate weight (continuous variable with double precision) weight <- create_con_var(   var = "weight",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 789 )  # View structure head(weight) weight 1 82.86145 2 41.08848 3 74.70480 4 77.74710 5 69.57973 6 67.73274 str(weight) 'data.frame':   1000 obs. of  1 variable:  $ weight: num  82.9 41.1 74.7 77.7 69.6 ... # Check the distribution summary(weight$weight) Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.   35.00   64.40   75.41   74.88   84.59  120.78 hist(weight$weight, breaks = 30, main = "Weight Distribution", xlab = "Weight (kg)", col = "lightgreen")

what: When to use individual functions vs create_mock_data()
title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: Use create_cat_var() create_con_var() : Testing single variable interactively Exploring distribution parameters Debugging metadata specifications Generating variables one time specific tests Use create_mock_data() : Generating complete datasets multiple variables Need variables generated together Production workflows saved metadata files Example: Batch generation Result: enabled variables generated single call, maintaining consistent sample size relationships.
code: # Generate multiple variables at once mock_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 100 )  # Check which variables were generated names(mock_data) [1] "age"            "smoking"        "BMI"            "height" [5] "weight"         "interview_date" head(mock_data[, c("smoking", "age", "weight")]) smoking age   weight 1       1  42 88.22128 2       1  52 69.28890 3       1  49 39.45189 4       2  63 87.50134 5       1  52 57.72547 6       2  55 63.62102

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: MockData supports two distributions continuous variables: common naturally-varying measurements: Parameters: distribution = "normal" mean - Center distribution sd - Spread (standard deviation) Examples: Age (mean = 50, sd = 15) Weight (mean = 75, sd = 15) Height (mean = 1.7, sd = 0.1) Properties: Bell-shaped curve Symmetric around mean ~68% within 1 SD, ~95% within 2 SD Values truncated valid range Equal probability across entire range: Parameters: distribution = "uniform" additional parameters needed Uses range variable_details.csv Example configuration: Use cases: ID numbers Random assignment codes Variables without known distribution
code: # variables.csv uid,variable,distribution v010,participant_id,uniform  # variable_details.csv uid,variable,recStart v010,participant_id,"[1000,9999]"

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: Proportions control population distribution categories. must sum 1.0 (±0.01 tolerance). proportions might reflect: Published health survey statistics Literature values population Target distributions study design proportion specification allows generate mock data matches “Table 1” descriptive statistics published papers. Example scenario: paper reports: Never smokers: 50% Former smokers: 30% Current smokers: 17% Don’t know: 3% setting proportions variable_details.csv, mock data match published distribution.

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: rType column variables.csv controls R data type output: factor (recommended): Preserves category levels Required statistical modeling Example: smoking status character: Plain text representation Use IDs non-analyzable categories Example: participant ID, study site integer: Whole numbers Use counts, age years Example: age, number children double: Decimal precision Use measurements requiring precision Example: weight, blood pressure, lab values Example comparison:
code: # Age: integer (no decimals) head(age$age) [1] 30 59 62 29 39 45 # Weight: double (decimal precision) head(weight$weight) [1] 82.86145 41.08848 74.70480 77.74710 69.57973 67.73274

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: Always verify generated data matches expectations: Validation checklist: Proportions sum ~1.0 (categorical) Values within expected range (continuous) Correct data type (factor, integer, double) Sample size matches n parameter Distribution shape looks reasonable (histogram)
code: # Check factor levels levels(smoking$smoking) [1] "1" "2" "3" "7" # Check proportions prop.table(table(smoking$smoking)) 1     2     3     7 0.505 0.305 0.160 0.030 # Verify data type class(smoking$smoking) [1] "factor" # Check range range(age$age, na.rm = TRUE) [1]  18 999 # Check distribution parameters mean(age$age, na.rm = TRUE) [1] 145.52 sd(age$age, na.rm = TRUE) [1] 284.5324 # Verify data type class(age$age) [1] "integer"

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: Validation checklist: Proportions sum ~1.0 (categorical) Values within expected range (continuous) Correct data type (factor, integer, double) Sample size matches n parameter Distribution shape looks reasonable (histogram)
code: # Check range range(age$age, na.rm = TRUE) [1]  18 999 # Check distribution parameters mean(age$age, na.rm = TRUE) [1] 145.52 sd(age$age, na.rm = TRUE) [1] 284.5324 # Verify data type class(age$age) [1] "integer"

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: tutorial, learned: Categorical variables: Using create_cat_var() generate variables discrete categories Proportions: specify validate category distributions Continuous variables: Using create_con_var() generate numeric measurements Distributions: Normal uniform distributions continuous data Output types: Choosing appropriate rType (factor, integer, double) variable Metadata structure: variables.csv variable_details.csv work together Individual vs batch: use single-variable functions vs create_mock_data() Validation: Checking generated data matches metadata specifications

title: Generating categorical and continuous variables
text: Continue learning: Date variables - Temporal data event times Survival data - Competing risks censoring Missing data - Survey missing codes (NA::vs NA::b) Garbage data - Data quality testing Reference: Configuration reference - Complete metadata schema Getting started - Overview first examples

title: Working with date variables
text: tutorial teaches temporal data generation MockData. ’ll learn create date variables : Cohort entry dates (index dates, baseline dates) Event dates (death, diagnosis, hospital admission) Administrative dates (censoring dates, data freeze dates) Temporal data quality testing (--period dates validation) Date variables use interval notation specify ranges support three distributions (uniform, Gompertz, exponential) realistic temporal patterns.

title: Working with date variables
text: Date variables use two-file structure variables, interval notation date ranges: variables.csv: variable_details.csv: Key points: Use interval notation recStart: [start_date,end_date] (square brackets, comma-separated, ISO format) Dates use ISO format: YYYY-MM-DD Single row per date variable (separate rows start/end) date variables minimal-example use ices_v* namespace Result: 100 interview dates uniformly distributed 2001-01-01 2005-12-31 (matching minimal-example metadata). Note: interval notation recStart specifies complete date range single row.
code: # Load minimal-example metadata variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  variable_details <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Generate interview dates interview_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 100,   seed = 123 )  # View interview date distribution head(interview_data$interview_date) [1] "2001-03-08" "2003-05-28" "2005-10-22" "2005-06-17" "2002-01-18" [6] "2005-04-24" summary(as.Date(interview_data$interview_date)) Min.      1st Qu.       Median         Mean      3rd Qu.         Max. "2001-01-17" "2002-01-02" "2002-11-14" "2003-03-06" "2004-05-07" "2005-12-12"

title: Working with date variables
text: interactive development testing, use create_date_var() generate single date variable without creating entire dataset: Use cases create_date_var(): Testing date specifications interactively Generating single variables specific tests Exploring distribution patterns Quick prototyping batch generation complete datasets: Use create_mock_data() generate variables (shown previous section).
code: # Generate just the interview_date variable interview_dates_only <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 100,   seed = 456 )  # View the result (single-column data frame) head(interview_dates_only) interview_date 1     2005-11-08 2     2005-02-17 3     2003-07-20 4     2002-07-04 5     2002-04-14 6     2004-06-29 summary(as.Date(interview_dates_only$interview_date)) Min.      1st Qu.       Median         Mean      3rd Qu.         Max. "2001-01-31" "2002-02-25" "2003-06-25" "2003-06-28" "2004-07-30" "2005-12-31"

title: Working with date variables
text: MockData supports three distributions date generation: Uniform (default): dates equally likely across range Use : Cohort accrual constant enrollment, administrative dates Example: distribution = "uniform" Gompertz: Useful survival/event times higher events near end range Use : Typical survival patterns Example: distribution = "gompertz" Exponential: events near start range Use : Time--event early concentration Example: distribution = "exponential" Note: Distribution types specified distribution column variables.csv. interactive generation (single variable functions), can override metadata distribution. Different distributions create different temporal patterns. ’s compare using date range: uniform distribution produces median date near middle range (2001-01-01 2005-12-31), expected evenly distributed dates. Distribution patterns: Choosing distribution: Start uniform calendar dates without known temporal patterns Use Gompertz mortality age-related outcomes Use exponential first-event early-diagnosis scenarios distribution read distribution column variables.csv, making easy specify different patterns different date variables.
code: # Generate dates with uniform distribution (default) uniform_dates <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 100 )  # Calculate median date median_uniform <- median(as.Date(uniform_dates$interview_date))  # View median median_uniform [1] "2003-09-10"

title: Working with date variables
text: generating event dates (death, diagnosis, hospitalization), individuals experience event follow-. event_prop parameter controls proportion individuals experience event versus censored (event observed). Let’s generate death dates 30% individuals die study period: Results: Deaths observed: 30 100 (30%) Censored (death): 70 (70%) Expected: 30% deaths (event_prop = 0.3) Sample first 10 observations (showing NA censored cases): Key concepts: event_prop = 0.3: 30% individuals experience event (get date) Censored observations: remaining 70% NA death_date (event observed) Survival analysis use: event_prop simulates realistic cohort data everyone experiences outcome use event_prop: Death dates cohort studies Disease incidence dates Hospital admission dates time--event outcome censoring occurs complete survival data examples competing risks multiple event types, see Survival data tutorial.
code: # Create simple death date configuration death_vars <- data.frame(   uid = "death_v1",   variable = "death_date",   role = "enabled",   variableType = "Date",   rType = "date",   position = 1,   distribution = "gompertz",   rate = 1e-04,   shape = 0.1,   followup_min = 0,   followup_max = 3650,   event_prop = 0.3,  # 30% experience death   sourceFormat = "analysis",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  death_details <- data.frame(   uid = "death_v1",   uid_detail = "death_v1_d1",   variable = "death_date",   recStart = "[2001-01-01,2005-12-31]",   recEnd = "copy",   catLabel = "Death dates",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Generate death dates (requires anchor_date for survival variables) anchor_dates <- as.Date("2001-01-01") + sample(0:1826, 100, replace = TRUE) df_mock <- data.frame(anchor_date = anchor_dates)  death_data <- create_date_var(   var = "death_date",   databaseStart = "tutorial",   variables = death_vars,   variable_details = death_details,   df_mock = df_mock,   n = 100,   seed = 456 )  # Check event proportion n_deaths <- sum(!is.na(death_data$death_date)) n_censored <- sum(is.na(death_data$death_date)) observed_prop <- n_deaths / nrow(death_data) # Display first 10 rows to show mixture of dates and NA values head(death_data, 10) death_date 1        <NA> 2        <NA> 3        <NA> 4  2003-12-30 5  2004-04-19 6        <NA> 7        <NA> 8        <NA> 9        <NA> 10       <NA>
