what: Source data format: simulating raw data imports
title: Working with date variables
text: default, MockData generates dates R Date objects (analysis-ready format). However, real survey data comes different formats depending source. sourceFormat column variables.csv controls dates generated simulate different raw data formats. MockData supports three sourceFormat formats specified variables.csv metadata: analysis (default): R Date objects ready analysis csv: Character strings ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD), simulating dates CSV files sas: Numeric values (days since 1960-01-01), simulating SAS date format sourceFormat column variables.csv controls output format. Let’s generate date variable three formats modifying metadata: Key insight: underlying dates represented three different formats. Using seed (100) ensures three formats contain dates, just stored differently. three formats represent identical dates. can convert : sourceFormat metadata column particularly useful testing date parsing harmonization code. Generate mock data format matching raw source files: Real survey data doesn’t arrive clean R Date objects: CSV files: Dates character strings need parsing SAS files: Dates may numeric (haven doesn’t auto-convert) SPSS/Stata files: Various numeric formats different epochs Setting sourceFormat variables.csv lets generate mock data matches actual raw data format, allowing test entire harmonization pipeline.
code: # Format 1: analysis (R Date objects) - DEFAULT # Variables already has sourceFormat = "analysis" by default dates_analysis <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 5,   seed = 100 )  # Check the class and values class(dates_analysis$interview_date) [1] "Date" head(dates_analysis$interview_date, 3) [1] "2005-10-04" "2002-05-18" "2004-10-13" # Format 2: csv (character strings) vars_csv <- variables vars_csv$sourceFormat <- "csv"  dates_csv <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_csv,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 5,   seed = 100  # Same seed = same underlying dates )  # Check the class and values class(dates_csv$interview_date) [1] "character" head(dates_csv$interview_date, 3) [1] "2005-10-04" "2002-05-18" "2004-10-13" # Format 3: sas (numeric days since 1960-01-01) vars_sas <- variables vars_sas$sourceFormat <- "sas"  dates_sas <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_sas,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 5,   seed = 100  # Same seed = same underlying dates )  # Check the class and values class(dates_sas$interview_date) [1] "numeric" head(dates_sas$interview_date, 3) [1] 16713 15478 16357 # Convert CSV format (character) to Date csv_to_date <- as.Date(dates_csv$interview_date)  # Convert SAS format (numeric) to Date sas_to_date <- as.Date(dates_sas$interview_date, origin = "1960-01-01")  # Verify all represent the same dates all(csv_to_date == dates_analysis$interview_date) [1] TRUE all(sas_to_date == dates_analysis$interview_date) [1] TRUE # Example: Testing harmonization from CSV source vars_csv_source <- variables vars_csv_source$sourceFormat <- "csv"  # Generate mock data in CSV format mock_csv <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_csv_source,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000 )  # Test your harmonization logic library(dplyr) harmonized <- mock_csv %>%   mutate(     # Parse character dates (your harmonization code)     interview_date = as.Date(interview_date, format = "%Y-%m-%d"),      # Calculate derived variables     days_from_start = as.numeric(interview_date - as.Date("2001-01-01"))   )  # Verify harmonization succeeded stopifnot(inherits(harmonized$interview_date, "Date")) stopifnot(all(harmonized$days_from_start >= 0, na.rm = TRUE))

title: Working with date variables
text: MockData supports three sourceFormat formats specified variables.csv metadata: analysis (default): R Date objects ready analysis csv: Character strings ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD), simulating dates CSV files sas: Numeric values (days since 1960-01-01), simulating SAS date format

title: Working with date variables
text: sourceFormat column variables.csv controls output format. Let’s generate date variable three formats modifying metadata: Key insight: underlying dates represented three different formats. Using seed (100) ensures three formats contain dates, just stored differently.
code: # Format 1: analysis (R Date objects) - DEFAULT # Variables already has sourceFormat = "analysis" by default dates_analysis <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 5,   seed = 100 )  # Check the class and values class(dates_analysis$interview_date) [1] "Date" head(dates_analysis$interview_date, 3) [1] "2005-10-04" "2002-05-18" "2004-10-13" # Format 2: csv (character strings) vars_csv <- variables vars_csv$sourceFormat <- "csv"  dates_csv <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_csv,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 5,   seed = 100  # Same seed = same underlying dates )  # Check the class and values class(dates_csv$interview_date) [1] "character" head(dates_csv$interview_date, 3) [1] "2005-10-04" "2002-05-18" "2004-10-13" # Format 3: sas (numeric days since 1960-01-01) vars_sas <- variables vars_sas$sourceFormat <- "sas"  dates_sas <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_sas,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 5,   seed = 100  # Same seed = same underlying dates )  # Check the class and values class(dates_sas$interview_date) [1] "numeric" head(dates_sas$interview_date, 3) [1] 16713 15478 16357

title: Working with date variables
text: three formats represent identical dates. can convert :
code: # Convert CSV format (character) to Date csv_to_date <- as.Date(dates_csv$interview_date)  # Convert SAS format (numeric) to Date sas_to_date <- as.Date(dates_sas$interview_date, origin = "1960-01-01")  # Verify all represent the same dates all(csv_to_date == dates_analysis$interview_date) [1] TRUE all(sas_to_date == dates_analysis$interview_date) [1] TRUE

title: Working with date variables
text: sourceFormat metadata column particularly useful testing date parsing harmonization code. Generate mock data format matching raw source files:
code: # Example: Testing harmonization from CSV source vars_csv_source <- variables vars_csv_source$sourceFormat <- "csv"  # Generate mock data in CSV format mock_csv <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_csv_source,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000 )  # Test your harmonization logic library(dplyr) harmonized <- mock_csv %>%   mutate(     # Parse character dates (your harmonization code)     interview_date = as.Date(interview_date, format = "%Y-%m-%d"),      # Calculate derived variables     days_from_start = as.numeric(interview_date - as.Date("2001-01-01"))   )  # Verify harmonization succeeded stopifnot(inherits(harmonized$interview_date, "Date")) stopifnot(all(harmonized$days_from_start >= 0, na.rm = TRUE))

title: Working with date variables
text: Real survey data doesn’t arrive clean R Date objects: CSV files: Dates character strings need parsing SAS files: Dates may numeric (haven doesn’t auto-convert) SPSS/Stata files: Various numeric formats different epochs Setting sourceFormat variables.csv lets generate mock data matches actual raw data format, allowing test entire harmonization pipeline.

title: Working with date variables
text: Simulate data entry errors dates future using garbage_high parameters variables.csv: Result: 20 birth dates (2%) future (2025-12-31), impossible current data. Sample future dates: 2026-01-18, 2026-05-28, 2026-08-02, 2026-11-24, 2027-02-09. Key insight: Garbage data now specified variable level variables.csv, variable_details.csv. Simulate impossibly old dates using garbage_low parameters: Result: 30 diagnosis dates (3%) 1950-01-01 (1850-1900 range), unrealistic modern medical data. Sample old dates: 1851-10-23, 1851-12-19, 1853-09-30, 1854-07-24, 1854-07-27. Testing validation pipelines: Ensure code catches impossible dates Training analysts: Show examples real-world data quality issues Data cleaning scripts: Test date range checks filtering logic Note: complete examples garbage date generation integrated metadata files, see minimal-example configuration files.
code: # Create configuration with future date garbage birth_config <- data.frame(   uid = "birth_date_v1",   variable = "birth_date",   role = "enabled",   variableType = "Date",   rType = "date",   position = 1,   garbage_high_prop = 0.02,   garbage_high_range = "[2026-01-01,2030-12-31]",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  birth_details <- data.frame(   uid = "birth_date_v1",   uid_detail = "birth_date_v1_d1",   variable = "birth_date",   recStart = "[1950-01-01,2010-12-31]",   catLabel = "Valid birth dates",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Write to temporary files temp_dir <- tempdir() birth_config_path <- file.path(temp_dir, "birth_config.csv") birth_details_path <- file.path(temp_dir, "birth_details.csv") write.csv(birth_config, birth_config_path, row.names = FALSE) write.csv(birth_details, birth_details_path, row.names = FALSE)  # Generate birth dates with future date garbage birth_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "tutorial",   variables = birth_config_path,   variable_details = birth_details_path,   n = 1000,   seed = 789 )  # Check for future dates (after 2025) future_threshold <- as.Date("2025-12-31") n_future <- sum(birth_data$birth_date > future_threshold, na.rm = TRUE) prop_future <- n_future / nrow(birth_data) future_dates_sample <- head(sort(birth_data$birth_date[birth_data$birth_date > future_threshold]), 5) # Create configuration with old date garbage diag_config <- data.frame(   uid = "diagnosis_date_v1",   variable = "diagnosis_date",   role = "enabled",   variableType = "Date",   rType = "date",   position = 1,   garbage_low_prop = 0.03,   garbage_low_range = "[1850-01-01,1900-12-31]",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  diag_details <- data.frame(   uid = "diagnosis_date_v1",   uid_detail = "diagnosis_date_v1_d1",   variable = "diagnosis_date",   recStart = "[2000-01-01,2020-12-31]",   catLabel = "Valid diagnosis dates",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Write to temporary files temp_dir <- tempdir() diag_config_path <- file.path(temp_dir, "diag_config.csv") diag_details_path <- file.path(temp_dir, "diag_details.csv") write.csv(diag_config, diag_config_path, row.names = FALSE) write.csv(diag_details, diag_details_path, row.names = FALSE)  # Generate diagnosis dates with old date garbage diag_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "tutorial",   variables = diag_config_path,   variable_details = diag_details_path,   n = 1000,   seed = 456 )  # Check for impossibly old dates (before 1950) old_threshold <- as.Date("1950-01-01") n_old <- sum(diag_data$diagnosis_date < old_threshold, na.rm = TRUE) prop_old <- n_old / nrow(diag_data) old_dates_sample <- head(sort(diag_data$diagnosis_date[diag_data$diagnosis_date < old_threshold]), 5)

title: Working with date variables
text: Simulate impossibly old dates using garbage_low parameters: Result: 30 diagnosis dates (3%) 1950-01-01 (1850-1900 range), unrealistic modern medical data. Sample old dates: 1851-10-23, 1851-12-19, 1853-09-30, 1854-07-24, 1854-07-27.
code: # Create configuration with old date garbage diag_config <- data.frame(   uid = "diagnosis_date_v1",   variable = "diagnosis_date",   role = "enabled",   variableType = "Date",   rType = "date",   position = 1,   garbage_low_prop = 0.03,   garbage_low_range = "[1850-01-01,1900-12-31]",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  diag_details <- data.frame(   uid = "diagnosis_date_v1",   uid_detail = "diagnosis_date_v1_d1",   variable = "diagnosis_date",   recStart = "[2000-01-01,2020-12-31]",   catLabel = "Valid diagnosis dates",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Write to temporary files temp_dir <- tempdir() diag_config_path <- file.path(temp_dir, "diag_config.csv") diag_details_path <- file.path(temp_dir, "diag_details.csv") write.csv(diag_config, diag_config_path, row.names = FALSE) write.csv(diag_details, diag_details_path, row.names = FALSE)  # Generate diagnosis dates with old date garbage diag_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "tutorial",   variables = diag_config_path,   variable_details = diag_details_path,   n = 1000,   seed = 456 )  # Check for impossibly old dates (before 1950) old_threshold <- as.Date("1950-01-01") n_old <- sum(diag_data$diagnosis_date < old_threshold, na.rm = TRUE) prop_old <- n_old / nrow(diag_data) old_dates_sample <- head(sort(diag_data$diagnosis_date[diag_data$diagnosis_date < old_threshold]), 5)

text: Testing validation pipelines: Ensure code catches impossible dates Training analysts: Show examples real-world data quality issues Data cleaning scripts: Test date range checks filtering logic Note: complete examples garbage date generation integrated metadata files, see minimal-example configuration files.

title: Working with date variables
text: generating dates, validate results: Validation results: Date range: 2001-01-17 2005-12-12 Missing dates: 0 dates within expected range (2001-01-01 2005-12-31): TRUE validation confirms generated dates match metadata specifications.
code: # Check interview date range interview_dates <- as.Date(interview_data$interview_date)  # Calculate summary statistics date_min <- min(interview_dates, na.rm = TRUE) date_max <- max(interview_dates, na.rm = TRUE) n_missing <- sum(is.na(interview_dates))  # Validate dates are within expected range (from metadata) expected_min <- as.Date("2001-01-01") expected_max <- as.Date("2005-12-31") all_valid <- all(interview_dates >= expected_min &                  interview_dates <= expected_max,                  na.rm = TRUE)

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: Data validation important research quality, know validation rules actually work? best approach generate mock data known quality issues, run validation pipeline, verify catches exactly expect. Health research projects often receive large datasets need robust validation pipelines catch data quality issues analysis. generating mock data intentional errors, can: Test validation logic applying real data Document expected error rates different quality checks Train new team members common data quality patterns Benchmark validation performance known error proportions Let’s start motivating example. Suppose ’re validating smoking status coding health survey dataset: clean data generated metadata, validation check finds 0 invalid codes. confirms data clean, know validation logic actually works?
code: # Load minimal-example metadata variable_details <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv",               package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv",               package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Generate smoking variable WITHOUT garbage (clean data only) df_clean <- data.frame() smoking_clean <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_clean,   n = 1000,   seed = 123 )  # Check for invalid codes (valid codes are 1=Never, 2=Former, 3=Current, 7=Don't know) # IMPORTANT: Exclude NA values - they are legitimate missing data, not garbage valid_codes <- c("1", "2", "3", "7") invalid_clean <- sum(!smoking_clean$smoking %in% valid_codes & !is.na(smoking_clean$smoking))

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: MockData uses unified garbage generation pattern across variable types (categorical, continuous, date, survival): Core pattern: - garbage_low_prop + garbage_low_range values valid range - garbage_high_prop + garbage_high_range values valid range - Specified variables metadata (added add_garbage() helper) Helper function easy setup:
code: # Add garbage to any variable type vars_with_garbage <- add_garbage(variables, "smoking",   garbage_low_prop = 0.02, garbage_low_range = "[-2, 0]")

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: Categorical variables can several types garbage data. common : Invalid codes: Values aren’t valid category set. Examples: “.”, “NA”, “missing”, numeric codes outside defined range. Type mismatches: Wrong data types. Example: Numeric codes stored floats (1.0 instead 1). Encoding issues: Character encoding problems. Example: “Montr0e9al” becomes “Montr”. Let’s demonstrate validation logic categorical variables. example shows properly validate categorical data excluding NA values (represent legitimate missing data, garbage): Validation results: Total records: 2000 Invalid codes found: 0 (0%) NA values (legitimate missing): 0 (0%) Since generated clean data without garbage specification, correctly find 0 invalid codes. NA values represent legitimate missing data patterns metadata proportions, garbage. Now let’s generate smoking data intentional invalid codes using garbage functions. smoking (valid codes: 1, 2, 3, 7), ’ll generate invalid codes valid range: Validation results: Total records: 1000 Garbage values found: 29 (2.9%) Expected: ~3% (garbage_low_prop = 0.03) Garbage codes generated: -2, -1, 0 functions generated invalid categorical codes (-2, -1, 0) treating valid codes ordinal. allows validation testing without pre-defining specific invalid category rows metadata. Instead using add_garbage(), can modify variables data frame directly: approaches produce identical results. Use add_garbage() cleaner code direct modification building programmatic workflows.
code: # Generate smoking variable (clean data from metadata) df_smoking <- data.frame() smoking_clean2 <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_smoking,   n = 2000,   seed = 789 )  # Validation: check for invalid codes # Get valid codes from metadata for smoking smoking_details <- variable_details[variable_details$variable == "smoking", ] valid_smoking_codes <- unique(smoking_details$recStart[!is.na(smoking_details$recStart)])  # CRITICAL: Exclude NA values - they are legitimate missing data, not garbage # Incorrect: invalid_smoking <- !smoking_clean2$smoking %in% valid_smoking_codes (counts NA as garbage) # Correct: invalid_smoking <- !smoking_clean2$smoking %in% valid_smoking_codes & !is.na(smoking_clean2$smoking) n_invalid <- sum(invalid_smoking) invalid_pct <- round(n_invalid / nrow(smoking_clean2) * 100, 1) # Add garbage to smoking using helper function vars_smoking_garbage <- add_garbage(variables, "smoking",   garbage_low_prop = 0.03, garbage_low_range = "[-2, 0]")  # Generate smoking with garbage smoking_garbage <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_smoking_garbage,  # Uses modified variables with garbage   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 999 )  # Validate: check for invalid codes # Get valid codes from metadata smoking_details_check <- variable_details[variable_details$variable == "smoking", ] valid_codes_check <- unique(smoking_details_check$recStart[!is.na(smoking_details_check$recStart)])  # Find garbage values (exclude NA - legitimate missing data) smoking_values <- as.numeric(as.character(smoking_garbage$smoking)) garbage_smoking <- smoking_values[smoking_values < 1 & !is.na(smoking_values)] n_garbage_smoking <- length(garbage_smoking) garbage_smoking_pct <- round(n_garbage_smoking / nrow(smoking_garbage) * 100, 1) # Direct modification approach (equivalent to add_garbage) vars_smoking_direct <- variables vars_smoking_direct$garbage_low_prop[vars_smoking_direct$variable == "smoking"] <- 0.03 vars_smoking_direct$garbage_low_range[vars_smoking_direct$variable == "smoking"] <- "[-2, 0]"  # Generate smoking with garbage (using directly modified variables) smoking_direct <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_smoking_direct,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 999  # Same seed as helper example )  # Validate: should match add_garbage() results smoking_direct_values <- as.numeric(as.character(smoking_direct$smoking)) garbage_direct <- smoking_direct_values[smoking_direct_values < 1 & !is.na(smoking_direct_values)]

NA values represent legitimate missing data patterns metadata proportions, garbage.

code: # Add garbage to smoking using helper function vars_smoking_garbage <- add_garbage(variables, "smoking",   garbage_low_prop = 0.03, garbage_low_range = "[-2, 0]")  # Generate smoking with garbage smoking_garbage <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_smoking_garbage,  # Uses modified variables with garbage   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 999 )  # Validate: check for invalid codes # Get valid codes from metadata smoking_details_check <- variable_details[variable_details$variable == "smoking", ] valid_codes_check <- unique(smoking_details_check$recStart[!is.na(smoking_details_check$recStart)])  # Find garbage values (exclude NA - legitimate missing data) smoking_values <- as.numeric(as.character(smoking_garbage$smoking)) garbage_smoking <- smoking_values[smoking_values < 1 & !is.na(smoking_values)] n_garbage_smoking <- length(garbage_smoking) garbage_smoking_pct <- round(n_garbage_smoking / nrow(smoking_garbage) * 100, 1)

code: # Direct modification approach (equivalent to add_garbage) vars_smoking_direct <- variables vars_smoking_direct$garbage_low_prop[vars_smoking_direct$variable == "smoking"] <- 0.03 vars_smoking_direct$garbage_low_range[vars_smoking_direct$variable == "smoking"] <- "[-2, 0]"  # Generate smoking with garbage (using directly modified variables) smoking_direct <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_smoking_direct,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 999  # Same seed as helper example )  # Validate: should match add_garbage() results smoking_direct_values <- as.numeric(as.character(smoking_direct$smoking)) garbage_direct <- smoking_direct_values[smoking_direct_values < 1 & !is.na(smoking_direct_values)]

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: Continuous variables different garbage patterns categorical variables. Common issues include: --range values: Numbers outside biologically/logically plausible ranges. Example: Age = 250 years. Type garbage data: Values stored wrong type. Example: Age stored character “45.5” instead numeric. Precision issues: Inappropriate decimal places. Example: Age = 45.7829 (overly precise). MockData generates continuous garbage using pattern: Use garbage_low_prop + garbage_low_range values valid range Use garbage_high_prop + garbage_high_range values valid range Specify exact ranges using interval notation (e.g., [-10, 0] [200, 300]) Let’s generate age data --range garbage. valid range 18-100 years, ’ll add high-range garbage 100 using add_garbage() helper: Validation results: Total records: 2000 Garbage values found: 36 (1.8%) Expected: ~2% (garbage_high_prop = 0.02) Sample garbage values: 120, 122, 124, 124, 125, 125, 127, 127 Value ranges: Overall range: 18 197 years Valid range (metadata): 18 100 Missing codes (metadata): 997, 998, 999 Key validation principle: Garbage values high-range (> 100) missing codes (997, 998, 999). confirms validator correctly excludes legitimate missing data garbage detection. Instead using add_garbage(), can modify variables data frame directly. approach useful building programmatic workflows: validator detects: Low-range garbage (< 18): 36 (1.8%) Minimum garbage value: -9 approaches (add_garbage() helper direct modification) produce identical results using parameters.
code: # Add high-range garbage to age using helper function vars_age_garbage <- add_garbage(variables, "age",   garbage_high_prop = 0.02, garbage_high_range = "[120, 200]")  # Generate age data with garbage df_age <- data.frame() age_garbage <- create_con_var(   var = "age",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_age_garbage,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_age,   n = 2000,   seed = 200 )  # CORRECT VALIDATION: Extract valid range and missing codes from metadata age_details <- variable_details[variable_details$variable == "age", ]  # Get valid range from metadata (where recEnd = "copy") valid_range_row <- age_details[age_details$recEnd == "copy", ] # Parse interval notation [18,100] to extract min and max library(stringr) range_str <- valid_range_row$recStart[1] range_values <- as.numeric(str_extract_all(range_str, "\\d+")[[1]]) valid_min <- range_values[1]  # 18 valid_max <- range_values[2]  # 100  # Get missing codes from metadata (where recEnd contains "NA::") missing_codes_rows <- age_details[grepl("NA::", age_details$recEnd), ] missing_codes <- as.numeric(missing_codes_rows$recStart)  # Validate: Garbage should be high-range (> 100) AND not a missing code is_garbage <- age_garbage$age > valid_max &               !age_garbage$age %in% missing_codes &               !is.na(age_garbage$age) n_garbage <- sum(is_garbage) garbage_pct <- round(n_garbage / nrow(age_garbage) * 100, 1)  # Display actual garbage values garbage_values <- age_garbage$age[is_garbage] # Direct modification approach - add low-range garbage (negative ages) vars_age_direct <- variables vars_age_direct$garbage_low_prop[vars_age_direct$variable == "age"] <- 0.02 vars_age_direct$garbage_low_range[vars_age_direct$variable == "age"] <- "[-10, 10]"  # Generate age data with low-range garbage df_age2 <- data.frame() age_direct <- create_con_var(   var = "age",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_age_direct,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_age2,   n = 2000,   seed = 201 )  # Validate: check for low-range garbage (below minimum valid age) low_range_garbage <- age_direct$age < 18 & !age_direct$age %in% c(997, 998, 999) n_low_garbage <- sum(low_range_garbage, na.rm = TRUE)

text: Let’s generate age data --range garbage. valid range 18-100 years, ’ll add high-range garbage 100 using add_garbage() helper: Validation results: Total records: 2000 Garbage values found: 36 (1.8%) Expected: ~2% (garbage_high_prop = 0.02) Sample garbage values: 120, 122, 124, 124, 125, 125, 127, 127 Value ranges: Overall range: 18 197 years Valid range (metadata): 18 100 Missing codes (metadata): 997, 998, 999 Key validation principle: Garbage values high-range (> 100) missing codes (997, 998, 999). confirms validator correctly excludes legitimate missing data garbage detection.
code: # Add high-range garbage to age using helper function vars_age_garbage <- add_garbage(variables, "age",   garbage_high_prop = 0.02, garbage_high_range = "[120, 200]")  # Generate age data with garbage df_age <- data.frame() age_garbage <- create_con_var(   var = "age",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_age_garbage,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_age,   n = 2000,   seed = 200 )  # CORRECT VALIDATION: Extract valid range and missing codes from metadata age_details <- variable_details[variable_details$variable == "age", ]  # Get valid range from metadata (where recEnd = "copy") valid_range_row <- age_details[age_details$recEnd == "copy", ] # Parse interval notation [18,100] to extract min and max library(stringr) range_str <- valid_range_row$recStart[1] range_values <- as.numeric(str_extract_all(range_str, "\\d+")[[1]]) valid_min <- range_values[1]  # 18 valid_max <- range_values[2]  # 100  # Get missing codes from metadata (where recEnd contains "NA::") missing_codes_rows <- age_details[grepl("NA::", age_details$recEnd), ] missing_codes <- as.numeric(missing_codes_rows$recStart)  # Validate: Garbage should be high-range (> 100) AND not a missing code is_garbage <- age_garbage$age > valid_max &               !age_garbage$age %in% missing_codes &               !is.na(age_garbage$age) n_garbage <- sum(is_garbage) garbage_pct <- round(n_garbage / nrow(age_garbage) * 100, 1)  # Display actual garbage values garbage_values <- age_garbage$age[is_garbage]

text: Instead using add_garbage(), can modify variables data frame directly. approach useful building programmatic workflows: validator detects: Low-range garbage (< 18): 36 (1.8%) Minimum garbage value: -9 approaches (add_garbage() helper direct modification) produce identical results using parameters.

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: Date variables use garbage generation pattern categorical continuous variables. Common date quality issues include: --range dates: Dates outside valid collection periods. Example: Interview date 2050. Impossible dates: Dates violate temporal logic. Example: Death date birth date. Format issues: Dates stored incorrectly. Example: “2020-13-45” (invalid month/day). Let’s generate interview dates far-future garbage dates test validation logic: Validation results: Total records: 1000 Future interview dates: 30 (3%) Expected: ~3% (garbage_high_prop = 0.03) can also modify variables data frame directly date garbage: approaches work identically date variables: Old interview dates (≤ 1990): 20 (2%) Expected: ~2% (garbage_low_prop = 0.02) advanced date generation techniques, see Date variables tutorial.
code: # Add high-range garbage to interview_date (future dates for testing) vars_date_garbage <- add_garbage(variables, "interview_date",   garbage_high_prop = 0.03, garbage_high_range = "[2030-01-01, 2050-12-31]")  # Generate interview dates with garbage df_dates <- data.frame() interview_garbage <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_date_garbage,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_dates,   n = 1000,   seed = 300 )  # Validate: check for future dates (impossible interviews) future_threshold <- as.Date("2030-01-01") future_interviews <- interview_garbage$interview_date > future_threshold n_future <- sum(future_interviews, na.rm = TRUE) future_pct <- round(n_future / nrow(interview_garbage) * 100, 1) # Direct modification - add low-range garbage (past dates) vars_date_direct <- variables vars_date_direct$garbage_low_prop[vars_date_direct$variable == "interview_date"] <- 0.02 vars_date_direct$garbage_low_range[vars_date_direct$variable == "interview_date"] <- "[1950-01-01, 1990-12-31]"  # Generate with low-range garbage interview_direct <- create_date_var(   var = "interview_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_date_direct,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 301 )  # Validate: check for very old dates old_threshold <- as.Date("1990-12-31") old_interviews <- interview_direct$interview_date <= old_threshold n_old <- sum(old_interviews, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: Creating raw survival data testing data cleaning pipelines Survival data tutorial teaches create clean, analysis-ready survival data correct temporal ordering. section teaches create raw survival data temporal violations testing data quality cleaning pipelines. Use approach need : - Test temporal validation logic (death entry, impossible dates) - Train analysts identify date sequence violations - Validate data cleaning scripts fix temporal inconsistencies Survival analysis requires coordinated date validation across multiple time points. Garbage data helps test complex validation rules temporal consistency. Common survival data quality issues: Date sequence violations: Death birth, death interview Impossible survival times: Negative follow-time, follow-exceeding study period Censoring inconsistencies: Status indicates death death date recorded prop_garbage parameter create_wide_survival_data() deprecated. Instead, add garbage individual date variables using functions: Validation detects 60 future death dates (3%), approximately matching 3% garbage specification. Key points survival garbage: create_wide_survival_data() creates clean, temporally-ordered survival data Add garbage individual date variables using add_garbage() helper create_date_var() (called internally) applies garbage automatically Test temporal validation checking impossible dates (e.g., far-future death dates) approach separates concerns: date-level garbage vs. survival data generation Temporal violations also produce invalid derived variables like impossibly long follow-times: Validation results derived variables: Impossibly long follow-time (> 20 years): 58 (2.9%) invalid follow-times correspond temporal violations (future garbage dates), confirming validators can catch derived quality issues.
code: # Define metadata (pass full data frames) surv_variables <- data.frame(   variable = c("study_entry", "death_date"),   variableType = c("Date", "Date"),   rType = c("date", "date"),   role = c("enabled", "enabled"),   distribution = c("uniform", "gompertz"),   rate = c(NA, 0.0001),   shape = c(NA, 0.1),   followup_min = c(NA, 30),   followup_max = c(NA, 3650),   event_prop = c(NA, 1.0),   sourceFormat = c("analysis", "analysis"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  surv_variable_details <- data.frame(   variable = c("study_entry", "death_date"),   recStart = c("[2010-01-01,2015-12-31]", "[30,3650]"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Add garbage to death_date (future dates for temporal violation testing) surv_vars_with_garbage <- add_garbage(surv_variables, "death_date",   garbage_high_prop = 0.03, garbage_high_range = "[2030-01-01, 2099-12-31]")  # Generate survival dates (create_date_var applies garbage automatically) survival_dates <- create_wide_survival_data(   var_entry_date = "study_entry",   var_event_date = "death_date",   var_death_date = NULL,   var_ltfu = NULL,   var_admin_censor = NULL,   databaseStart = "test",   variables = surv_vars_with_garbage,  # Uses modified variables with garbage   variable_details = surv_variable_details,   n = 2000,   seed = 400 )  # Validate: check for impossibly future death dates (temporal violation proxy) future_threshold <- as.Date("2030-01-01") future_deaths <- survival_dates$death_date > future_threshold n_violations <- sum(future_deaths, na.rm = TRUE) # Calculate follow-up time in days (entry to event) survival_dates$followup_days <- as.numeric(   difftime(     survival_dates$death_date,     survival_dates$study_entry,     units = "days"   ) )  # Validate: follow-up should be within reasonable range (max 20 years = 7300 days) max_reasonable_followup <- 7300 invalid_followup <- survival_dates$followup_days > max_reasonable_followup n_invalid_fu <- sum(invalid_followup, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: Temporal violations also produce invalid derived variables like impossibly long follow-times: Validation results derived variables: Impossibly long follow-time (> 20 years): 58 (2.9%) invalid follow-times correspond temporal violations (future garbage dates), confirming validators can catch derived quality issues.
code: # Calculate follow-up time in days (entry to event) survival_dates$followup_days <- as.numeric(   difftime(     survival_dates$death_date,     survival_dates$study_entry,     units = "days"   ) )  # Validate: follow-up should be within reasonable range (max 20 years = 7300 days) max_reasonable_followup <- 7300 invalid_followup <- survival_dates$followup_days > max_reasonable_followup n_invalid_fu <- sum(invalid_followup, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Generating garbage data for validation testing
text: Now understand individual garbage patterns, let’s build complete validation pipeline tests quality checks systematically. ’ll use MockData functions generate dataset multiple garbage types: validation suite detects intentional garbage patterns exist dataset, confirming validator works correctly.
code: # Generate complete dataset with multiple garbage types using create_mock_data() full_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv", package = "MockData"),   variable_details = system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv", package = "MockData"),   n = 5000,   seed = 500 )  # Run validation suite # Check 1: Categorical codes (smoking) - exclude NA values smoking_details <- variable_details[variable_details$variable == "smoking", ] valid_smoking <- unique(smoking_details$recStart[!is.na(smoking_details$recStart)]) smoking_invalid <- !full_data$smoking %in% valid_smoking & !is.na(full_data$smoking)  # Check 2: Age range (valid: 18-100, excluding missing codes) age_invalid <- (full_data$age < 18 | full_data$age > 100) & !full_data$age %in% c(997, 998, 999)  # Check 3: Combined validation (any record with any issue) any_issue <- smoking_invalid | age_invalid  # Build results table validation_results <- data.frame(   check = c("smoking: invalid codes", "age: out of range", "Any validation failure"),   n_fail = c(sum(smoking_invalid), sum(age_invalid, na.rm = TRUE), sum(any_issue, na.rm = TRUE)),   pct_fail = c(     round(sum(smoking_invalid) / nrow(full_data) * 100, 2),     round(sum(age_invalid, na.rm = TRUE) / nrow(full_data) * 100, 2),     round(sum(any_issue, na.rm = TRUE) / nrow(full_data) * 100, 2)   ) )  # Display results validation_results check n_fail pct_fail 1 smoking: invalid codes      0        0 2      age: out of range      0        0 3 Any validation failure      0        0

what: Understanding missing data in health surveys
title: Missing data in health surveys
text: Health surveys use structured missing data codes distinguish different types non-response. Unlike general R data NA represents missing value, survey data differentiates several categories missingness. distinction crucial accurate statistical analysis. Consider simple example: calculating smoking prevalence survey data. treat missing values way, ’ll get biased estimates. Let’s see . Now let’s calculate prevalence ways: naive approach gives us prevalence 22.4%, correct approach gives 22%. difference matters making population-level estimates comparing across surveys. health surveys, missing values mean thing. Canadian health surveys like Canadian Community Health Survey (CCHS) Canadian Health Measures Survey (CHMS) use standardized systems missing data codes: Valid skip (996): question asked skip logic determined wasn’t applicable Don’t know (997): question asked respondent didn’t know answer Refusal (998): question asked respondent refused answer stated (999): question asked response recorded type different statistical implications. Valid skips excluded denominator (weren’t part eligible population question). don’t know, refusal, stated included denominator calculating response rates, even though ’re excluded numerator calculating prevalence. Let’s generate realistic data see action: Health surveys categorize missing data three main types, requiring different statistical treatment. Valid skip (code 996) valid skip occurs skip logic determines question asked. example, someone reports ’ve never smoked, won’t asked “many cigarettes per day smoke?” isn’t truly missing data—’s logical consequence previous answer. Statistical treatment: Exclude numerator denominator. respondents weren’t eligible question. Don’t know / Refusal / stated (codes 997, 998, 999) codes represent questions asked didn’t receive valid responses: 997 (Don’t know): Respondent uncertain answer 998 (Refusal): Respondent declined answer 999 (stated): Question asked response recorded Statistical treatment: Include denominator calculating response rates (eligible asked), exclude numerator calculating prevalence (don’t know true status). applicable (code 996) similar valid skip—question doesn’t apply respondent’s situation. statistical treatment valid skip. Let’s demonstrate difference worked example using smoking status: dataset: Sample size: 1000 respondents Asked: 1000 (respondents variable) Response rate: 97% (valid responses ÷ asked) Current smoking prevalence: 19.5% (current smokers ÷ asked) Don’t know responses: 30 (3% asked) Notice “Don’t know” (code 7) included denominator calculating response rate excluded numerator calculating current smoking prevalence. Let’s look BMI variable, demonstrates valid skip (NA::) missing codes (NA::b): shows missing codes appear survey metadata. recStart column contains category codes, including continuous range ([15,50]), valid skip (996 applicable, mapped NA::), missing data codes ([997,999] don’t know/refusal/stated, mapped NA::b). can use metadata generate mock data realistic proportions: age variable: Sample size: 5000 respondents Response rate: 90% (4500 valid responses ÷ 5000 asked) Mean age (valid responses ): 50.1 years Don’t know responses: 247 (4.9% asked) Refusal: 146 (2.9% asked) stated: 107 (2.1% asked) demonstrates health survey data includes measurable proportions missing data codes, distinguishing matters accurate statistical reporting.
code: # Generate smoking data with two approaches set.seed(123)  # Wrong approach: treating all missing as NA smoking_wrong <- data.frame(   smoker = sample(c("Yes", "No", NA), 1000,                   replace = TRUE,                   prob = c(0.20, 0.70, 0.10)) )  # Correct approach: using survey missing codes smoking_correct <- data.frame(   smoker = sample(c("1", "2", "6", "7", "9"), 1000,                   replace = TRUE,                   prob = c(0.20, 0.70, 0.05, 0.03, 0.02)) ) # Wrong calculation (naive approach) wrong_prevalence <- mean(smoking_wrong$smoker == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE)  # Correct calculation (excluding valid skip, including DK/RF/NS in denominator) valid_responses <- smoking_correct$smoker %in% c("1", "2", "7", "9") correct_prevalence <- sum(smoking_correct$smoker == "1") / sum(valid_responses) # Load metadata for examples variable_details <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv",               package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv",               package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Create smoking data using MockData # smoking has categories: 1=Never, 2=Former, 3=Current, 7=Don't know df_mock <- data.frame() smoking_data <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_mock,   n = 1000,   seed = 456 )  # Show distribution table(smoking_data$smoking) 1   2   3   7 466 309 195  30 # Calculate response rate (includes DK in denominator, excludes any skip codes) asked <- smoking_data$smoking %in% c("1", "2", "3", "7") valid_response <- smoking_data$smoking %in% c("1", "2", "3") response_rate <- sum(valid_response) / sum(asked)  # Calculate prevalence of current smoking (excludes DK from numerator, but includes in denominator) current_smoker <- smoking_data$smoking == "3" prevalence <- sum(current_smoker) / sum(asked) # Look at BMI variable metadata bmi_details <- variable_details[variable_details$variable == "BMI", ] print(bmi_details[, c("variable", "recStart", "recEnd", "catLabel", "proportion")], row.names = FALSE) variable  recStart recEnd                        catLabel proportion       BMI   [15,50]   copy                 Valid BMI range         NA       BMI       996  NA::a                  Not applicable        0.3       BMI [997,999]  NA::b Don't know, refusal, not stated        0.1 # Generate age data with missing codes df_mock_age <- data.frame() age_data <- create_con_var(   var = "age",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   df_mock = df_mock_age,   n = 5000,   seed = 789 )  # Calculate response rate excluding missing codes valid_ages <- !is.na(age_data$age) & age_data$age >= 18 & age_data$age <= 100 missing_codes <- age_data$age %in% c(997, 998, 999) asked_age <- valid_ages | missing_codes response_rate_age <- sum(valid_ages) / sum(asked_age)  # Summary statistics on valid ages only mean_age <- mean(age_data$age[valid_ages], na.rm = TRUE)

what: The recEnd column: Enabling missing data classification
title: Missing data in health surveys
text: generate realistic missing data, MockData needs distinguish valid response codes (1, 2, 3) missing data codes (6-9, 96-99). recEnd column variable_details.csv provides classification. follow recodeflow approach supports tagged NA using haven packages. recodeflow can add tagged_NA recoding tranforming variables. NA::assigned ‘applicable’ NA::b assing ‘missing/refused/stated’. recEnd column maps input codes classification: Valid responses: Map codes using numeric values Missing codes: Use NA::b don’t know, refusal, stated Skip codes: Use NA::valid skip/applicable Without recEnd, MockData tell difference : Code 1 (valid: Daily smoker) Code 7 (missing: Don’t know) just numbers recStart. recEnd column provides explicit classification: recEnd = "1" → Valid response code recEnd = "NA::b" → Missing data (DK/Refusal/NS) recEnd = "NA::" → Skip code (applicable) recEnd column conditionally required variable_details.csv contains missing data codes (6-9, 96-99): Validation error: use codes like 7, 8, 9 recStart without recEnd column, ’ll get error: recEnd optional: simple variables without missing codes, recEnd can omitted: configuration tells MockData: Codes 1, 2, 3 valid responses (recEnd maps ) Code 7 missing code (recEnd = “NA::b”) generating data, 3% “Don’t know” responses MockData uses recodeflow’s NA:: convention missing data classification: NA::(skip codes): Question asked due skip logic Example: “smoked last 30 days?” skipped never smokers Statistical treatment: Exclude denominator (eligible) Common codes: 6, 96, 996 NA::b (missing codes): Question asked valid response given Includes: Don’t know (7, 97), Refusal (8, 98), stated (9, 99) Statistical treatment: Include denominator response rates Common codes: 7-9, 97-99 convention enables automatic missing data handling throughout MockData’s generation functions.
code: # variable_details.csv uid,uid_detail,variable,recStart,recEnd,catLabel,proportion v_002,d_002,smoking,1,1,Daily smoker,0.25 v_002,d_003,smoking,2,2,Occasional,0.15 v_002,d_004,smoking,3,3,Never,0.57 # variable_details.csv (continued) v_002,d_005,smoking,7,NA::b,Don't know,0.03 v_002,d_006,smoking,6,NA::a,Valid skip,0.01 recEnd column required in variable_details when using missing data codes (6-9, 96-99).   Use 'NA::a' for skip codes (6, 96, 996),   Use 'NA::b' for missing codes (7-9, 97-99) representing DK/Refusal/NS,   and numeric codes (e.g., '1', '2', '3') for valid responses. # Simple categorical variable (no missing codes) - recEnd optional uid,uid_detail,variable,recStart,catLabel,proportion v_010,d_100,gender,1,Male,0.5 v_010,d_101,gender,2,Female,0.5 # Create variable_details with recEnd column smoking_details_recEnd <- data.frame(   uid = "cchsflow_v0002",   uid_detail = c("cchsflow_d00005", "cchsflow_d00006", "cchsflow_d00007", "cchsflow_d00008"),   variable = "smoking",   recStart = c("1", "2", "3", "7"),   recEnd = c("1", "2", "3", "NA::b"),  # Explicit classification   catLabel = c("Never smoker", "Former smoker", "Current smoker", "Don't know"),   proportion = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.17, 0.03),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Show the configuration print(smoking_details_recEnd, row.names = FALSE) uid      uid_detail variable recStart recEnd       catLabel  cchsflow_v0002 cchsflow_d00005  smoking        1      1   Never smoker  cchsflow_v0002 cchsflow_d00006  smoking        2      2  Former smoker  cchsflow_v0002 cchsflow_d00007  smoking        3      3 Current smoker  cchsflow_v0002 cchsflow_d00008  smoking        7  NA::b     Don't know  proportion        0.50        0.30        0.17        0.03

Use 'NA::a' for skip codes (6, 96, 996),   Use 'NA::b' for missing codes (7-9, 97-99) representing DK/Refusal/NS,   and numeric codes (e.g., '1', '2', '3') for valid responses. # Simple categorical variable (no missing codes) - recEnd optional uid,uid_detail,variable,recStart,catLabel,proportion v_010,d_100,gender,1,Male,0.5 v_010,d_101,gender,2,Female,0.5

title: Missing data in health surveys
text: MockData uses recodeflow’s NA:: convention missing data classification: NA::(skip codes): Question asked due skip logic Example: “smoked last 30 days?” skipped never smokers Statistical treatment: Exclude denominator (eligible) Common codes: 6, 96, 996 NA::b (missing codes): Question asked valid response given Includes: Don’t know (7, 97), Refusal (8, 98), stated (9, 99) Statistical treatment: Include denominator response rates Common codes: 7-9, 97-99 convention enables automatic missing data handling throughout MockData’s generation functions.

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: Survival analysis requires careful coordination multiple date variables strict temporal ordering: Cohort entry date (baseline, index date) Event dates (disease incidence, outcomes interest) Competing risks (death prevents observation primary event) Censoring events (loss follow-, administrative censoring) MockData’s create_wide_survival_data() generates dates proper temporal constraints realistic distributions. tutorial: Clean survival data correct temporal ordering tutorial teaches create meaningful, analysis-ready survival data correct temporal ordering. dates follow proper survival analysis constraints (entry ≤ event, death competing risk, etc.). data quality testing: need generate raw data temporal violations (e.g., death entry, impossible dates) testing data cleaning pipelines, see Garbage data tutorial covers survival-specific garbage patterns.

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: simplest survival data two dates: cohort entry single event. Result: row interview date (cohort entry) primary event date. event dates NA (censored - event occur follow-). individuals experience primary event. event_prop parameter variables.csv controls event occurrence rate: Configuration: primary_event_date variable event_prop = 0.3 variables.csv, meaning 30% individuals experience event. Observed: 300 1000 individuals (30%) experienced primary event.
code: # Load minimal-example metadata variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   check.names = FALSE )  variable_details <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variable_details.csv", package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   check.names = FALSE )  # Generate entry + event dates surv_basic <- create_wide_survival_data(   var_entry_date = "interview_date",   var_event_date = "primary_event_date", # disease incidence or similar   var_death_date = NULL,   var_ltfu = NULL,  # Loss to follow-up   var_admin_censor = NULL, # i.e. End of study follow-up   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 123 )  # View first few rows head(surv_basic) interview_date primary_event_date 1     2002-02-19               <NA> 2     2002-04-08         2002-05-23 3     2001-06-28               <NA> 4     2002-06-10               <NA> 5     2001-07-14               <NA> 6     2003-07-27               <NA>

title: Generating survival data with competing risks

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: Death competing risk - individuals die experience primary event. MockData handles temporal logic automatically. MockData applies rules: Entry date baseline: dates occur entry Death prevents events: death < event, set event NA (observe event death) Temporal ordering: interview_date ≤ event_date, interview_date ≤ death_date Temporal ordering validation: events occur entry: TRUE deaths occur entry: TRUE confirms MockData correctly enforces temporal constraints.
code: # Generate entry + event + death surv_compete <- create_wide_survival_data(   var_entry_date = "interview_date",   var_event_date = "primary_event_date",   var_death_date = "death_date",   var_ltfu = NULL,   var_admin_censor = NULL,   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 456 )  # Check temporal ordering head(surv_compete[, c("interview_date", "primary_event_date", "death_date")]) interview_date primary_event_date death_date 1     2005-11-08         2006-01-17       <NA> 2     2005-02-17               <NA>       <NA> 3     2003-07-20               <NA>       <NA> 4     2002-07-04               <NA>       <NA> 5     2002-04-14         2002-06-07       <NA> 6     2004-06-29         2004-09-13       <NA> # Verify temporal ordering # Note: Dates are already R Date objects (sourceFormat = "analysis" in variables.csv) interview_dates <- surv_compete$interview_date event_dates <- surv_compete$primary_event_date death_dates <- surv_compete$death_date  # Check: All events occur after entry all_events_after_entry <- all(   event_dates[!is.na(event_dates)] >= interview_dates[!is.na(event_dates)],   na.rm = TRUE )  # Check: All deaths occur after entry all_deaths_after_entry <- all(   death_dates[!is.na(death_dates)] >= interview_dates[!is.na(death_dates)],   na.rm = TRUE )

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: MockData applies rules: Entry date baseline: dates occur entry Death prevents events: death < event, set event NA (observe event death) Temporal ordering: interview_date ≤ event_date, interview_date ≤ death_date Temporal ordering validation: events occur entry: TRUE deaths occur entry: TRUE confirms MockData correctly enforces temporal constraints.
code: # Verify temporal ordering # Note: Dates are already R Date objects (sourceFormat = "analysis" in variables.csv) interview_dates <- surv_compete$interview_date event_dates <- surv_compete$primary_event_date death_dates <- surv_compete$death_date  # Check: All events occur after entry all_events_after_entry <- all(   event_dates[!is.na(event_dates)] >= interview_dates[!is.na(event_dates)],   na.rm = TRUE )  # Check: All deaths occur after entry all_deaths_after_entry <- all(   death_dates[!is.na(death_dates)] >= interview_dates[!is.na(death_dates)],   na.rm = TRUE )

what: Complete survival data: entry + event + death + censoring
title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: Real cohort studies multiple censoring mechanisms: Loss follow-: Participants drop Administrative censoring: Study ends specific date
code: # Generate complete survival data (all 5 date variables) surv_complete <- create_wide_survival_data(   var_entry_date = "interview_date",   var_event_date = "primary_event_date",   var_death_date = "death_date",   var_ltfu = "ltfu_date",   var_admin_censor = "admin_censor_date",   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 2000,   seed = 789 )  # View structure head(surv_complete) interview_date primary_event_date death_date  ltfu_date admin_censor_date 1     2003-03-24               <NA>       <NA>       <NA>        2020-04-12 2     2003-02-19               <NA>       <NA> 2020-02-15        2010-05-26 3     2005-03-14         2005-05-16 2006-03-14       <NA>        2024-11-15 4     2004-08-14               <NA>       <NA>       <NA>        2018-07-07 5     2002-10-28         2003-01-16       <NA>       <NA>        2017-10-07 6     2002-09-16               <NA>       <NA>       <NA>        2017-10-29

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: Survival analysis requires deriving time--event event indicators date variables. Observation ends earliest : primary event, death, loss follow-, administrative censoring. Event indicator identifies observation ended: 0: Censored (loss follow-administrative censoring) 1: Primary event occurred 2: Death occurred (competing risk) Result: Censored (0): 1132 (56.6%) - lost follow-administratively censored Primary event (1): 582 (29.1%) - experienced primary event Death (2): 286 (14.3%) - died primary event
code: # Dates are already R Date objects (sourceFormat = "analysis") # Calculate end date (earliest of all outcomes) # Build list of existing date columns date_cols <- c("primary_event_date", "death_date", "ltfu_date", "admin_censor_date") existing_cols <- date_cols[date_cols %in% names(surv_complete)]  # Calculate minimum date across existing columns if (length(existing_cols) > 0) {   # Use row-wise minimum to avoid pmin Inf issues with all-NA rows   surv_complete$t_end <- as.Date(     apply(surv_complete[, existing_cols, drop = FALSE], 1, function(row_dates) {       valid_dates <- row_dates[!is.na(row_dates)]       if (length(valid_dates) == 0) return(NA_real_)       min(valid_dates)     }),     origin = "1970-01-01"   ) } else {   surv_complete$t_end <- as.Date(NA) }  # Calculate follow-up time in days # Date subtraction returns difftime object; convert to numeric days surv_complete$followup_days <- as.numeric(difftime(surv_complete$t_end, surv_complete$interview_date, units = "days"))  head(surv_complete[, c("interview_date", "t_end", "followup_days")]) interview_date      t_end followup_days 1     2003-03-24 2020-04-12          6229 2     2003-02-19 2010-05-26          2653 3     2005-03-14 2005-05-16            63 4     2004-08-14 2018-07-07          5075 5     2002-10-28 2003-01-16            80 6     2002-09-16 2017-10-29          5522 # Create event indicator surv_complete$event_indicator <- ifelse(   !is.na(surv_complete$primary_event_date) & surv_complete$primary_event_date == surv_complete$t_end, 1,  # Event   ifelse(!is.na(surv_complete$death_date) & surv_complete$death_date == surv_complete$t_end, 2,  # Death   0)  # Censored )  # Tabulate outcomes table(surv_complete$event_indicator) 0    1    2 1132  582  286

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: Observation ends earliest : primary event, death, loss follow-, administrative censoring.

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: Survival dates use realistic distributions match real-world patterns: Uniform: Constant hazard (administrative censoring, loss follow-) Gompertz: Age-dependent hazard (death, chronic disease) Exponential: Constant hazard early concentration Distribution parameters specified variables.csv used automatically create_wide_survival_data().
code: distribution = "uniform" distribution = "gompertz" rate = 0.0001 shape = 0.1 distribution = "exponential" rate = 0.001

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: default, survival dates generated R Date objects (sourceFormat = "analysis"). However, can simulate different raw data formats using sourceFormat column variables.csv: Available sourceFormat values: analysis (default): R Date objects ready analysis csv: Character strings ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD), simulating CSV file imports sas: Numeric values (days since 1960-01-01), simulating SAS date format matters: Real cohort data doesn’t arrive clean R Date objects: CSV files survey instruments contain character dates requiring parsing SAS files may numeric dates need conversion Different data sources require different harmonization approaches Example: Testing different source formats sourceFormat value variables.csv controls output format. Let’s generate interview_date SAS numeric format keeping dates analysis format: Result: interview_date column numeric (days since 1960-01-01), primary_event_date remains Date object. simulates mixed-format raw data requires harmonization. convert SAS dates R Date format:
code: # Modify only interview_date to use SAS format vars_sas <- variables vars_sas$sourceFormat[vars_sas$variable == "interview_date"] <- "sas"  surv_sas <- create_wide_survival_data(   var_entry_date = "interview_date",   var_event_date = "primary_event_date",   var_death_date = NULL,   var_ltfu = NULL,   var_admin_censor = NULL,   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars_sas,  # Modified to use SAS format for interview_date   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 100,   seed = 123 )  # Check the format: interview_date is numeric (SAS), others are Date head(surv_sas) interview_date primary_event_date 1          15390         2012-05-06 2          15438               <NA> 3          15154         2011-09-15 4          15501               <NA> 5          15170               <NA> 6          15913               <NA> # Convert SAS numeric dates to R Date interview_converted <- as.Date(surv_sas$interview_date, origin = "1960-01-01") head(interview_converted) [1] "2002-02-19" "2002-04-08" "2001-06-28" "2002-06-10" "2001-07-14" [6] "2003-07-27"

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: minimal-example metadata includes temporal violations garbage_high_prop garbage_high_range parameters. generate future dates violate temporal constraints testing validation pipelines: Validation detects: 14 temporal violations 1000 observations (1.4%). future dates (> 2025-01-01) represent data quality issues validation pipeline flag. Breakdown: Future primary events: 8 Future deaths: 6 demonstrates MockData’s garbage parameters help test validation logic generating realistic data quality issues.
code: # Generate survival data with configured garbage dates surv_qa <- create_wide_survival_data(   var_entry_date = "interview_date",   var_event_date = "primary_event_date",   var_death_date = "death_date",   var_ltfu = NULL,   var_admin_censor = NULL,   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 999 )  # Check for temporal violations # Look for events that occur after impossibly far in the future (2025+) future_threshold <- as.Date("2025-01-01")  # Count future dates in primary_event_date n_future_events <- sum(surv_qa$primary_event_date > future_threshold, na.rm = TRUE)  # Count future dates in death_date n_future_deaths <- sum(surv_qa$death_date > future_threshold, na.rm = TRUE)  # Total violations n_violations <- n_future_events + n_future_deaths prop_violations <- round(100 * n_violations / nrow(surv_qa), 1)

title: Generating survival data with competing risks
text: tutorial, learned: Basic survival generation: Entry date + event date event proportions Competing risks: Death competing event prevents primary event observation Temporal constraints: MockData enforces proper date ordering Complete cohort data: Entry + event + death + loss--follow-+ administrative censoring Derived variables: Creating follow-time event indicators raw dates Distributions: Using Gompertz, uniform, exponential realistic temporal patterns QA testing: Generating temporal violations validate data quality pipelines

title: Generate Mock Data from Metadata Specifications
text: Status: Experimental, pre-release software MockData work--progress R package generating mock testing data small metadata specifications. useful today development documentation workflows, especially paired recodeflow-style metadata (see ), treated experimental infrastructure rather stable released package. People using MockData reporting helpful. take encouraging signal, evidence package mature. Please review generated data using workflow matters. MockData can currently help : Generate metadata-driven mock variables development testing Use inline metadata small examples, without creating separate CSV files Create categorical, continuous, date-like fields simple specifications Add intentional invalid --range “garbage” values QA testing Generate basic time--event style mock dates survival-analysis workflows Support examples tutorials without exposing real person-level data Current development limitations: APIs may change formal release Error handling permissive can fail warnings instead stopping test suite yet cover every important edge case Generated data manually checked intended metadata rules 30-second standalone example quick numeric variable, create_con_var() can use two small metadata data frames.
code: library(MockData)  variables <- data.frame(   variable = "age",   label = "Age",   variableType = "Continuous",   rType = "integer",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  variable_details <- data.frame(   variable = "age",   recStart = "[18,85]", # range for generated values   recEnd = "copy", # use recStart as the generation rule   proportion = 1,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  age <- create_con_var(   var = "age",   databaseStart = "example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 100,   seed = 123 )  head(age) #>   age #> 1  37 #> 2  71 #> 3  45 #> 4  77 #> 5  81 #> 6  21

what: Standalone example with a continuous age range
title: Generate Mock Data from Metadata Specifications
text: , rType = "double" keeps age continuous numeric value variable_details defines valid age range.
code: library(MockData)  variables <- data.frame(   variable = "age",   label = "Age",   variableType = "Continuous",   rType = "double",   role = "enabled",   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  variable_details <- data.frame(   variable = "age",   recStart = "[18,85]",   recEnd = "copy",   proportion = 1,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  age <- create_con_var(   var = "age",   databaseStart = "example",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 100,   seed = 123 )  head(age) #>        age #> 1 37.26769 #> 2 70.81644 #> 3 45.40145 #> 4 77.16217 #> 5 81.01131 #> 6 21.05229

title: Generate Mock Data from Metadata Specifications
text: package, “mock data” refers metadata-driven simulated data created solely software testing workflow validation. Mock data generated variable specifications contain real person-level data identifiable information. Key distinctions: Mock data (package): Generated metadata . Mimics variable structure ranges real-world statistical relationships. Used testing pipelines, analysis. Synthetic data: Preserves statistical properties relationships real datasets generative models. May used research properly validated. Dummy data: Placeholder minimal test data, often hardcoded randomly generated without metadata constraints. MockData creates data looks realistic (appropriate variable types, value ranges, category labels, tagged NAs) relationship actual population. Joint distributions correlations purposely differ real-world data. MockData used test data analyses pipelines, including data transformation, cleaning, analyses, modelling. example, mockData allow create range invalid data (call ‘garbage’ data) test data cleaning processes.

title: Generate Mock Data from Metadata Specifications
text: MockData uses three-file architecture separates project data dictionaries, study specifications, MockData-specific parameters:

title: Generate Mock Data from Metadata Specifications
text: Project data dictionary (variables.csv) Variable names, labels, types, role flags Shared across analysis projects Example: variable, variableType, label Study specifications (variable_details.csv) Study date ranges, follow-periods Category definitions value ranges Transformation rules (recStart, recEnd, copy, catLabel) Example: uvariable, recStart, catLabel MockData-specific parameters (mock_config.csv, optional) Proportions variable categories Event occurrence probabilities (event_occurs) Distribution parameters (distribution) Garbage data proportions (prop_invalid) Advanced features (survival data, data quality testing) separation allows MockData read existing recodeflow metadata files without modification, supporting optional MockData-specific configurations needed.

what: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
text: Helper function add garbage data specifications variables data frame. provides convenient way specify invalid/garbage values quality assurance testing. Works consistently across variable types (categorical, continuous, date).

title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
code: add_garbage(   variables,   var,   garbage_low_prop = NULL,   garbage_low_range = NULL,   garbage_high_prop = NULL,   garbage_high_range = NULL )

title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
text: variables Data frame variable metadata (typically read variables.csv) var Character. Variable name add garbage specifications . Must exist variables$variable. garbage_low_prop Numeric. Proportion observations generate low-range garbage (0-1). NULL, low-range garbage added. garbage_low_range Character. Interval notation specifying range low-range garbage values (e.g., [-2, 0] categorical, [0, 1.4) continuous, [1900-01-01, 1950-12-31] dates). NULL, low-range garbage added. garbage_high_prop Numeric. Proportion observations generate high-range garbage (0-1). NULL, high-range garbage added. garbage_high_range Character. Interval notation specifying range high-range garbage values (e.g., [10, 15] categorical, [60, 150] continuous, [2025-01-01, 2099-12-31] dates). NULL, high-range garbage added.

title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
text: Modified variables data frame garbage specifications added. garbage columns exist, created initialized NA variables.

title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
text: variable types use garbage specification pattern: garbage_low_prop + garbage_low_range values valid range garbage_high_prop + garbage_high_range values valid range

title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
text: Categorical (ordinal treatment):   Continuous:   Date:
code: # Valid codes: 1, 2, 3, 7 # Generate codes -2, -1, 0 below valid range vars <- add_garbage(vars, "smoking",   garbage_low_prop = 0.02, garbage_low_range = "[-2, 0]") # Valid range: [18, 100] # Generate extreme ages above valid range vars <- add_garbage(vars, "age",   garbage_high_prop = 0.03, garbage_high_range = "[150, 200]") # Valid range: [2000-01-01, 2020-12-31] # Generate future dates for QA testing vars <- add_garbage(vars, "death_date",   garbage_high_prop = 0.03, garbage_high_range = "[2025-01-01, 2099-12-31]")

title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
text: function returns modified variables data frame, making pipe-friendly:
code: vars_with_garbage <- variables %>%   add_garbage("age", garbage_high_prop = 0.03, garbage_high_range = "[150, 200]") %>%   add_garbage("smoking", garbage_low_prop = 0.02, garbage_low_range = "[-2, 0]") %>%   add_garbage("death_date", garbage_high_prop = 0.03,     garbage_high_range = "[2025-01-01, 2099-12-31]")

title: Add garbage specifications to variables data frame — add_garbage
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Load metadata variables <- read.csv(   system.file("extdata/minimal-example/variables.csv",     package = "MockData"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE, check.names = FALSE )  # Add garbage to age (high-range only) vars <- add_garbage(variables, "age",   garbage_high_prop = 0.03, garbage_high_range = "[150, 200]")  # Add garbage to smoking (low-range only) vars <- add_garbage(vars, "smoking",   garbage_low_prop = 0.02, garbage_low_range = "[-2, 0]")  # Add garbage to BMI (two-sided invalid values) vars <- add_garbage(vars, "BMI",   garbage_low_prop = 0.02, garbage_low_range = "[-10, 15)",   garbage_high_prop = 0.01, garbage_high_range = "[60, 150]")  # Generate data with garbage mock_data <- create_mock_data(   databaseStart = "minimal-example",   variables = vars,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 123 ) } # }

what: Apply garbage data from variables.csv — apply_garbage
title: Apply garbage data from variables.csv — apply_garbage
text: Applies garbage data using parameters variables.csv extension columns. Garbage data specified variable level.

title: Apply garbage data from variables.csv — apply_garbage
code: apply_garbage(   values,   var_row,   variable_type,   missing_codes = NULL,   seed = NULL )

title: Apply garbage data from variables.csv — apply_garbage
text: values Vector. Generated values (already valid + missing). var_row Data frame. Single row variables.csv (contains garbage data parameters). variable_type Character. "categorical", "continuous", "integer", "date". missing_codes Numeric vector. Optional. Missing codes extracted metadata (rows recEnd contains "NA::"). Used exclude missing codes valid range calculations. NULL, uses hardcoded fallback backward compatibility. seed Integer. Optional random seed.

title: Apply garbage data from variables.csv — apply_garbage
text: Vector garbage data applied.

title: Apply garbage data from variables.csv — apply_garbage
text: Garbage data fields (variables.csv): Garbage parameters: garbage_low_prop: Proportion low garbage values (0-1) garbage_low_range: Range low values (interval notation [min,max]) garbage_high_prop: Proportion high garbage values (0-1) garbage_high_range: Range high values (interval notation [min,max]) Application order: Apply garbage_low (specified) Apply garbage_high (specified) overlap - indices removed application Config-driven generation: var_row NULL missing garbage fields, garbage data applied.

title: Apply garbage data from variables.csv — apply_garbage
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ var_row <- data.frame(   variable = "BMI",   garbage_low_prop = 0.02,   garbage_low_range = "[-10,0]",   garbage_high_prop = 0.01,   garbage_high_range = "[60,150]" ) values <- c(23.5, 45.2, 30.1, 18.9, 25.6) result <- apply_garbage(values, var_row, "continuous", seed = 123) } # }

text: rType column missing, defaults applied based variable type: continuous/cont → "double" categorical/cat → "factor" date → "Date" logical → "logical" Unknown → "character"

title: Apply rType defaults to variable details — apply_rtype_defaults
text: "integer": Whole numbers (age, counts, years) "double": Decimal numbers (BMI, income, percentages) "factor": Categorical levels "character": Text codes "logical": TRUE/FALSE values "Date": Date objects "POSIXct": Datetime objects

title: Apply rType defaults to variable details — apply_rtype_defaults
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Missing rType - defaults applied details <- data.frame(   variable = "age",   typeEnd = "cont",   recStart = "[18, 100]" ) details <- apply_rtype_defaults(details) # details$rType is now "double"  # Existing rType - preserved details <- data.frame(   variable = "age",   typeEnd = "cont",   rType = "integer" ) details <- apply_rtype_defaults(details) # details$rType remains "integer" } # }

text: var character. Variable name generate (column name output) databaseStart character. Database/cycle identifier filtering metadata (e.g., "cchs2001_p", "minimal-example"). Used filter variables variable_details specified database. variables data.frame character. Variable-level metadata containing: variable: Variable names database: Database identifier (optional, filtering) rType: R output type (factor/character/integer/logical) garbage_low_prop, garbage_high_prop: Garbage data parameters Can also file path (character) variables.csv. variable_details data.frame character. Detail-level metadata containing: variable: Variable name (joining) recStart: Category code range recEnd: Classification (numeric code, "NA::", "NA::b") proportion: Category proportion (0-1, must sum 1) catLabel: Category label/description Can also file path (character) variable_details.csv. df_mock data.frame. Optional. Existing mock data (check variable already exists). prop_missing numeric. Proportion missing values (0-1). Default 0 (missing). > 0, function looks rows recEnd containing "NA::" variable_details. n integer. Number observations generate. seed integer. Optional. Random seed reproducibility.

title: Create categorical variable for MockData — create_cat_var
text: v0.3.0 API: function now accepts full metadata data frames filters internally specified variable database. "recodeflow pattern" filtering handled inside function. Generation process: Filter metadata: Extract rows specified var + database Extract proportions: Read variable_details (proportion column) Generate population: Sample categories based proportions Apply missing codes: prop_missing > 0 proportions metadata Apply garbage: Read garbage parameters variables.csv Apply rType: Coerce specified R type (factor/character/integer/logical) Type coercion (rType): rType column variables.csv controls output data type: "factor": Factor levels category codes (default categorical) "character": Character vector "integer": Integer (numeric category codes) "logical": Logical (TRUE/FALSE categories) Missing data: Missing codes identified recEnd containing "NA::": NA::: Skip codes (applicable) NA::b: Missing codes (know, refusal, stated) Proportions missing codes read proportion column variable_details. Garbage data: Garbage parameters read variables.csv: garbage_low_prop, garbage_low_range: -range invalid values garbage_high_prop, garbage_high_range: -range invalid values

title: Create categorical variable for MockData — create_cat_var
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Basic usage with metadata data frames smoking <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000,   seed = 123 )  # Expected output: data.frame with 1000 rows, 1 column ("smoking") # Values: Factor with levels from metadata (e.g., "1", "2", "3", "7") # Distribution: Based on proportions in variable_details # Example: #   smoking # 1       1 # 2       3 # 3       2 # 4       1 # 5       7 # ...  # With missing data (uses proportions from metadata) smoking <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = variables,   variable_details = variable_details,   n = 1000 ) # Missing codes (recEnd = "NA::b") automatically included based on proportions  # With file paths instead of data frames result <- create_cat_var(   var = "smoking",   databaseStart = "cchs2001_p",   variables = "path/to/variables.csv",   variable_details = "path/to/variable_details.csv",   n = 1000 ) } # }

title: Create continuous variable for MockData — create_con_var

Generation process: Filter metadata: Extract rows specified var + database Extract distribution parameters: Read variables.csv Extract valid range: Parse variable_details recStart column Generate population: Based distribution type (uniform/normal/exponential) Apply missing codes: proportions specified metadata Apply garbage: Read garbage parameters variables.csv Apply rType: Coerce specified R type (integer/double) Type coercion (rType): rType column variables.csv controls output data type: \"integer\": Rounds converts integer (age, counts) \"double\": Double precision (default continuous) Distribution types: \"uniform\": Uniform distribution [min, max] recStart \"normal\": Normal distribution (requires mean, sd variables.csv) \"exponential\": Exponential distribution (requires rate variables.csv) Missing data: Missing codes identified recEnd containing "NA::": NA::: Skip codes (applicable) NA::b: Missing codes (know, refusal, stated) Garbage data: Garbage parameters read variables.csv: garbage_low_prop, garbage_low_range: -range invalid values garbage_high_prop, garbage_high_range: -range invalid values
